neverland-system/cues/to-audition.html.description:
Tenses (v): auditioned (past), audition (present), auditioning (progressive)
Laconic: To create an Actor for the Troupe.To audition aMechanically,see alsoWhenMechanically,

neverland-system/cues/to-breed.html.title: to breed
neverland-system/cues/to-breed.html.description: Tenses (v): (past), (present), (progressive) Laconic:To verb aMechanically,see alsoWhen Mechanically, (Crossbred) implies that an Actor has two or more Species (Dynamic) Lines. When Directors consider an Actor's Features, they should remember to consider all of their Species (Dynamic) Lines.

neverland-system/cues/to-burn.html.title: to burn
neverland-system/cues/to-burn.html.description:
Tenses (v): burned (past), burn (present), burning (progressive) Laconic: To ensure the success of something.To burn a Statement is to ensure that Arbitration will be unfairly biased towards that Statement. Whatever the Results of Arbitration may be, improve them by one stage; a Dull Result becomes a Dim Result, and a Dim Result becomes a Bright result. If a Result is already Bright, the result remains Bright.
Even burning a Statement will not magically make it logically valid. If a burned Statement cannot be true because it violates Suspension of Disbelief or Discomfort, Directors are encouraged to melt or wick the Statement into something more defensible, with the Table's consent. If the Table does not consent to melting an invalid Statement, it might not become (Canonized).
see also (Snuffed) (antonym), as well as its corollaries (Wicked) and (Melted)
Whenever a Statement is burned, it is introduced with (Burned); this Feature ought to be denoted when introduced as it has a very important relevance to Arbitration. (Burned) Statements are especially vulnerable to both wicking and melting. A Statement which is both (Snuffed) and (Burned) is neither; both Features are to be estranged at the Director's earliest convenience, allowing the Statement to be introduced to a second snuffing or burning.

neverland-system/cues/to-canonize.html.title: to canonize
neverland-system/cues/to-canonize.html.description:
Tenses (v): canonized (past), canonize (present), canonizing (progressive) Laconic: To move a Statement from the Rack to Canon.To canonize a Statement is to play it to Canon and remove it from the Rack. In order to be canonized, a Statement must first exist on the Rack. To start the process of canonizing, Directors may close the Conflict State and ask for a response from the Table. If no Players respond with a relevant Statement that challenges the Statement being canonized within a reasonable time-frame, then the Statement is removed from the Rack and played to Canon.
see also (Established) (synonym) and (Disputed) (antonym)
Statements are implied to have this Feature introduced at the precise moment they are moved into Canon. (Canonized) Statements as a rule behave more like Facts than Fictions. They may still be treated as Opinions or Alternatives, but must also be considered as valid interpretations of reality.

neverland-system/cues/to-cast.html.title: to cast
neverland-system/cues/to-cast.html.description:
Tenses (v): (past), (present), (progressive) Laconic: Using a Cue or Strike to accomplish a goal.To cast is to use a Cue or Strike to gain a mechanical benefit. By playing a Cue or a Strike against a Target (likely followed by a Statement), a Player may manipulate the Rack and Canon.
Casting is different from making a Statement alone; casting implies the use of Rule of Magical Thinking and abstraction. A Statement which is the result of a cast can be easily melted and wicked, and is much less likely to be logically (Valid) because it often lacks the Threads that would connect logical conclusions together. Casting accomplishes with magical brute force what a deft and charismatic hand could without relying on any other rules or mechanics.see also
Whenever a Statement is produced by a Cue or a Strike, it is implied to also have the (Cast) Feature. This Feature makes it more vulnerable to Arbitration as a rule, but is unlikely to be relevant to Arbitration in the course of play.
Clever Directors are encouraged to use the (Cast) Feature to denote that an Actor or Prop is the product of Rule of Magical Thinking or abstraction and that the Actor (or Prop) is a stand-in for something else. Magical illusions and allusions are easier to foreshadow if you keep their Features in mind.

neverland-system/cues/to-denote.html.title: to denote
neverland-system/cues/to-denote.html.description:
Tenses (v): denoted (past), denote (present), denoting (progressive) Laconic: To write something down for clarity.To denote, a Player at the Table takes note of something they've observed and pockets that Note. The Note may be introduced to an existing Statement to make it more clear for other Players. Directors are encouraged to denote all relevant Features attached to a Statement, as well as keep their own Notes in a journal or writing software the Table cannot easily witness.Note-taking has shown positive benefits for long-term memory and mental health, so we encourage all Players to get into the habit of taking notes!see also
When a Feature or Statement has been properly (Denoted), there should be a visible stamp, marker or Thread properly linking the Feature or Statement and the Note. Placing the Note in the same Pocket as the subject of the Note is usually sufficient to consider a Feature or Statement to be (Denoted).

neverland-system/cues/to-diffuse.html.title: to diffuse
neverland-system/cues/to-diffuse.html.description:
Tenses (v): (past), (present), (progressive) Laconic: To snuff one's own Results to help another Actor.To diffuse a Result is to give up on a selfish victory for the benefit of others. Diffusing could be as simple as doubling back to save a rival's life, essentially giving up on the opportunity to win in exchange for something far more valuable. Diffusing can also be used to represent teamwork, essentially 'spending' one's own effort to make another Actor look like a hero.
Since diffusing requires sacrifice, Casters must always name their sacrifice
Mechanically, diffusing snuffs a Bright or Dim Result to burn another Result. Diffusing is likely to pin Casters, so Casters are discouraged from diffusing their own Statements.
Diffusing may be cast during Arbitration without additional cost, but sacrificing a (Canonized) Statement to cast this Cue will return a Dull Result. Dull Results will almost always leave the Caster (Pinned), so diffuse wisely.see alsoWhen
Mechanically, (Diffused) Results should be treated as if they were snuffed for all intents and purposes. A Bright Result is treated as a Dim Result, and a Dim Result should be treated as a Dull Result.

neverland-system/cues/to-encourage.html.title: to encourage
neverland-system/cues/to-encourage.html.description: Tenses (v): (past), (present), (progressive) Laconic:To verb aMechanically, see alsoWhenMechanically, (Encouraged) Cues are burned by the Neverlands System for Directors (and, to a lesser extent, Players). The Scribes have done extensive

neverland-system/cues/to-enjoin.html.title: to enjoin
neverland-system/cues/to-enjoin.html.description:
Tenses (v): enjoined (past), enjoin (present), enjoining (infinitive) Laconic: Join a Conflict.To enjoin a Conflict is to make a Statement to the Rack during an Open Conflict State. Sarcastic and witty remarks do not automatically put an Actor in Conflict with other Actors, but Directors may request an Actor join Conflict if it becomes clear they are attempting to strategize or help other Players at the Table during a Conflict they have not enjoined yet.
When an Actor enjoins a Conflict, they must also claim a Position. The Position they claim should be placed at the top of the Rack as an Alternative to a Foundation in Canon, and the Directors should canonize this Alternative in a Closed State before returning to an Open State. In simpler terms: Actors need to pick where they stand on the Stage after enjoining.
When inflicted as a Condition, Enjoined forces an Actor to enjoin into Conflict, even if they didn't want to do so in the first place. Players must still consent to joining the Conflict. When an Enjoined Actor enjoins combat, they may take the Foundation in Canon as their Position or present an Alternative to the Rack. Enjoined Actors can still be relocated from their relatively safe position, so they might need to defend the Foundation against Strikes and Statements.

neverland-system/cues/to-gaslight.html.title: to gaslight
neverland-system/cues/to-gaslight.html.description:
Tenses (v): gaslit (past), gaslight (present), gaslighting (progressive) Laconic: To make someone believe they are insane.To gaslight an Actor is to tell them lies to make them doubt their own story. Gaslighting should never be done against Players; Players are intended to have all observable knowledge of the plot at all times. Players may choose not to observe a Fact, and they may choose to believe in Fiction or Opinions, but the Table should avoid misleading one another. In Conflict, however, gaslighting is encouraged for sneaky and manipulative Actors.
To gaslight another Actor, simply rebut a Statement in play with a simple 'I don't believe that's what happened.' Playing this Opinion is hard to defend against; the other Actors who witnessed the event will need to produce testimony to prove that the Actor who holds that Opinion lacks credibility, or to reveal evidence which shows the event happened as described.
Gaslighting is an affirmative defense against most Statements. If the produced Opinion is canonized, it will snuff any Links on the Rack. The only way to avoid getting snuffed after being gaslit is to play to the Sink instead!see also
When a Statement has been introduced with (Gaslit), it and all links are effectively snuffed until more evidence has been revealed. Alternatively, testimony may be used to mitigate (Gaslit) by showing the Opinions of other Actors to be dubious evidence against your claim.

neverland-system/cues/to-hold.html.title: to hold
neverland-system/cues/to-hold.html.description:
Tenses (v): held (past), hold (present), holding (progressive) Laconic: To take a Position.To hold a Position is for an Actor to take a definitive stance that their Opinion is that a Position is a valid Fact. Actors believe in this Position so strongly that they are willing to defend all linked Positions from as well.
To relocate and hold a new Position implies the Actor is willing to be pinned to that Position if they cannot properly defend it. Every Actor holds at least one Position at the start of a Conflict, and if they hold no Position at any point during Conflict they are given a response window in which they can hold a new Position; if they cannot grasp a new Position, they are (Pinned).
see also (Estranged) (antonym) and its corollary (Relocated)
When a Position is (Held), then it is held by at least one Actor in the Scene. So long as any Actor holds a Position, they are implied to have introduced (Held).

neverland-system/cues/to-imply.html.title: to imply
neverland-system/cues/to-imply.html.description:
Tenses (v): implied (past), imply (present), implying (progressive) Laconic: To remind others of what already exists.To imply a Statement or Feature is present is to suggest that it has always been present, even if improperly denoted. This is different from gaslighting in that the Caster could make a logically valid argument for the presence of the implied Target; assuming charitability, the subject of the implication was likely not made evident out of haste, not malice.
It would certainly slow down the pace of the game if each Statement needed to be critically analyzed and broken down into tokenized sub-Statements and sub-Features; some amount of implication is necessary to keep Suspension of Disbelief and the Theory of Fun alive.
With that being said, Directors are encouraged to obfuscate mysteries and use implication as a technique to avoid foreshadowing their mysteries too early. Directors do not need to tell the Table that the vagrant they've been traveling with is secretly the prince of an ancient kingdom; it should be possible to imply as such without stating the truth outright. When the Table presents a logically valid argument, Directors are strongly encouraged by the Theory of Honesty to reveal the truth.see also
When a Feature or Statement is (Implied), that means the Statement or Feature is present even if the Table has not yet denoted its presence. It is likely common sense that this critical and relevant mechanic is present even if it has not been denoted; if it seems easier to denote the (Implied) Feature or Statement from this point onward, Directors are encouraged to do so!

neverland-system/cues/to-improvise.html.title: to improvise
neverland-system/cues/to-improvise.html.description:
Tenses (v): improvised (past), improvise (present), improvising (progressive) Laconic: To make changes to the mechanics for any reason.To improvise a Scene is to discard existing Game Mechanics in favor of different rules or mechanics. Sometimes, the Rules as Written are too restrictive to apply; generally speaking, if there are no risky actions taking place, mechanics are not necessary. Improvising is taking a step beyond that; the Directors can invent their own rules for Arbitration on the spot if it would be more reasonable than following the rules.
For example, a Director might bring out figurines to help them visualize the Scene for other Players. They might resolve Statements on the Rack using the figurines; they could argue an Actor was too far away from a downed foe to pocket their weapon, or that their line of sight prevented them from observing a stealthy foe. These tools are not included in the Neverlands System, but we acknowledge they are genuinely useful for some cases and encourage Directors to look for ways to increase immersion.
Lastly, any use of improvisation should follow the Rule of Consistency. It is important that Players are able to predict how Cues will play out in advance.asee also
When an Element is (Improvised), it was introduced by the Table using Game Mechanics not present in the Neverlands System. (Improvised) Elements should be carefully scrutinized by Directors during Arbitration to ensure they follow the Rule of Consistency.

neverland-system/cues/to-introduce.html.title: to introduce
neverland-system/cues/to-introduce.html.description:
Tenses (v): introduced (past), introduce (present), introducing (infinitive)To introduce a Feature is to combine an Element with a Dynamic. Most often, introduction is used to place a new Minor Dynamics in a Pocket, even one currently occupied by a Prop. Introduction must be approved by a Director, since some Dynamics are incompatible with some Props; we leave determining the inappropriate combinations as an exercise for the reader, since Dynamics are both very broad and open to interpretation. There could be any possible number of justifications for allowing a combination of Prop and Dynamic under one circumstance but not another.
The antonym effect of introduce is to estrange.
An (Introduced) Prop is one that has a Minor Dynamic or an additional Major Dynamic. Props do not always have Minor Dynamics, and props which lack Minor Dynamics are considered to be (Estranged) Props, even if they have a Major Dynamic. There are few circumstances under which a Prop could have multiple Dynamics, as most are powerful and should not be combined; we leave this problem as another exercise for the reader.

neverland-system/cues/to-melt.html.title: to melt
neverland-system/cues/to-melt.html.description:
Tenses (v): melted (past), melt (present), melting (progressive) Laconic: to simplify a concept To melt a Statement is to reduce it into another form. Typically, melting will create an Alternative and play it to the Racks during an Open Conflict State; the Player is presenting an alternative hypothesis for how an event occurred. Melted Statements are usually simplified, lacking Threads to Alternative and relying purely on Lines.
Ultimately, the Player who made the targeted Statement has final say as to whether or not a Melted Statement is accepted into Canon, so long as their objection is based on the rules of Discomfort. If there is no Discomfort, the targeted Player defers judgement to the Director;
see also (Wicked) (antonym), as well as its corollaries (Snuffed) and (Burned)
Whenever a Statement is melted, it is introduced with (Melted); this Feature ought to be denoted when introduced, as it signals there is a linked Alternative to this Feature in Canon or on the Rack. (Melted) Statements are Alternatives in addition to their other Major Dynamics; they represent a possible interpretation of the same events. When the Statement referenced by a (Melted) Alternative is removed from the Rack, the Directors should close the Conflict State and immediately resolve the (Melted) Alternative.

neverland-system/cues/to-play.html.title: to play
neverland-system/cues/to-play.html.description:
Tenses (v): played (past), play (present), playing (progressive) Laconic: To put something into play.To play an Element of the Neverlands System is to place that Element in the Rack or Canon. Playing an Element creates a Statement which also implies that the Element exists and should be reviewed by the Table. The Directors have the final say as to whether or not an Element should be canonized after being played.
see also (Pocketed) (antonym)
When an Element is played for the first time, it is implied to have the (Played) Feature. This Feature is unlikely to be relevant as all Elements in play have this Feature, but the Feature should be denoted if it is ever logical to do such.

neverland-system/cues/to-present.html.title: to present
neverland-system/cues/to-present.html.description:
Tenses (v): presented (past), present (present), presenting(progressive)
Laconic:To verb asee alsoWhen

neverland-system/cues/to-profile.html.title: to profile
neverland-system/cues/to-profile.html.description: Tenses (v): (past), (present), (progressive) Laconic:To profile Actors is to denote useful context on one of their Lines. In other words, profiling is the Praxis of creating a Line for an Actor.Mechanically,see alsoWhenMechanically,

neverland-system/cues/to-resolve.html.title: to resolve
neverland-system/cues/to-resolve.html.description:
Tenses (v): resolved (past), resolve (present), resolving (progressive) Laconic: Figuring out if something is Canon.To resolve a Statement is to decide how a Statement is canonized or rejected from Canon. During resolution, the Table and the Directors decide whether or not the top Statement of the Rack makes sense, given the current state of Canon. In other words, the Directors must resolve Arbitration.
While Statements are resolving, the Directors should close the Conflict State. If the Statement has no challenges and seems logically valid, then it should be accepted into Canon. If the Directors feel like there might be some caveats, they'll raise concerns in this step, melting or wicking a Statement as necessary to get clarifying information. If the Director thinks a challenge is credible, they should reject the Statement; if appropriate, they should return that Prop to the appropriate Pocket.
Whatever the Result of Arbitration, when the Directors are done resolving a Statement they should open the Conflict State once more.see also
When a Statement has been resolved, it is implied to have (Resolved) introduced as a Feature. This is a self-documenting Feature, as when a Statement is canonized it will be in Canon. Any other Result will see the Statement leave the Rack and potentially return to a Pocket.

neverland-system/cues/to-respond.html.title: to respond
neverland-system/cues/to-respond.html.description:
Tenses (v): responded (past), respond (present), responding (progressive) Laconic: To read a Statement.To respond to a Statement is to directly acknowledge a Statement and to follow up the acknowledged Statement with another Statement, a Cue or a Strike. Players are encouraged to respond to most or all relevant Statements.
Responding is a staple mechanic of the Neverlands System that the entire Table should be comfortable with; ignoring Statements made by other Players is impolite, if not downright detrimental to the success of the Narrative. Reading the Statements others make is a basic courtesy to them!see also
When a Player responds to a Statement, the Director has been implied to have introduced the (Responded) Feature to that Statement. It is not a relevant Feature in normal play, but can be useful to denote when the Table is unsure if they have already addressed a Statement directly and there are many Statements to parse through on the Rack and in Canon.

neverland-system/cues/to-sever.html.title: to sever
neverland-system/cues/to-sever.html.description:
Tenses (v): severed (past), sever (present), severing (progressive)
Laconic: To destroy a Thread.To verb asee alsoWhen

neverland-system/cues/to-sink.html.title: to sink
neverland-system/cues/to-sink.html.description:
Disambiguation: Sinks (the Pocket)Tenses (v): sinked (past), sink (present), sinking (progressive) Laconic: To threaten someone with a Prop or Cue.
To sink a Statement is to play it to the Sink, a special Pocket that each Actor enjoined to Conflict possesses. Sinking a Statement prevents other Players from manipulating the Statement in any way, shape or form and introduces the (Sinked) Feature to that Statement.see also
When a Statement has been (Sinked), it is now immune to manipulation for as long as the Statement remains in the Sink. When a (Sinked) Statement in the Sink Pocket is resolved, it is both safe and burned; it might as well be canonized!

neverland-system/cues/to-stake.html.title: to stake
neverland-system/cues/to-stake.html.description:
Tenses (v): staked (past), stake (present), staking (progressive) Laconic: To raise the stakes of Conflict. To stake an Element is to offer it and raise the stakes of Conflicts. This is a Cue which requires consent from the other Players at the Table, but it is an effective way to subvert an Apocalypse; by staking, an Actor effectively offers an all-or-nothing gamble to the other Cult.
Pinning Actors while the stakes are raised will result in catastrophic losses for their Cult, so Actors are often filled with determination they might not usually have. This is almost certainly the climax of the Act; remember that any staked Elements will be divided among all remaining Cults in Conflict equitably.
Mechanically, targeted Elements are introduced with (Staked) and every (Enjoined) Cult in Conflict is required to stake Assets of similar collective value to the (Targeted) Elements. The Directors weigh each offering, and if the stakes seem reasonable and the Table consents, the staked Assets are also introduced with (Staked).
see also Assets and Dances
When (Staked) is introduced to a Statement or Prop, then those Elements are now up for grabs in the current Conflict. If any Cult no longer has Actors in the Conflict, they lose all claims to everything which is (Raised); the remaining Cults will secure their Assets equitably.
Mechanically, (Staked) Assets in Canon snuff any attempts to pin Cultists that are part of the Cult which currently possesses said Assets. When Conflict ends, all (Staked) Assets and Elements are distributed equitably among the Cults left standing; the Directors arbitrate the Results.

neverland-system/cues/to-subvert.html.title: to subvert
neverland-system/cues/to-subvert.html.description:
Tenses (v): subverted (past), subvert (present), subverting (progressive) Laconic: To stop an Apocalypse or undo an Accord.To subvert an Apocalypse or Accord is to undermine the premise. To subvert an Accord, you need to make an Apocalypse happen; such a big change to the rules of a Dragon-Temple will surely draw the attention and ire of those looking to maintain the status quo, especially Priestesses and Cultists who respect the desires of their Dragon-Temple.
(Fascists may also take issue with any attempts to change or improve life, though their reasons are much less comprehensible.)
Once an Apocalypse or Accord is subverted, the Table shouldn't have to worry about the opposing Cult trying their plan a second time; the Cult which tried to cast the Apocalypse will feel discouraged and return to the drawing board. There may be future attempts, but it is increasingly unlikely they will try again in the same way! Likewise, subverting an Accord will make it hard to consider a second attempt as Priestesses usually respect the will of their Dragon-Temple, even when Dragon-Temple makes unhealthy decisions.
Mechanically, targeted Elements should be considered; if the Apocalypse ends in a Bright Result, nothing happens. If the Apocalypse ends in a Dull Result, that Apocalypse (and any related Accords) should be introduced with (Subverted).
If the Apocalypse ends in a Dim Result, the Directors should carefully consider which parts of the Apocalypse were successful and which parts were not; they should rewrite the initial Apocalypse as different, smaller Apocalypses. Directors may introduce (Canonized) to one set of Apocalypses and Accords, and (Subverted) to the other Apocalypses and Accords.
see also Accords and Dances
When an Accord or Apocalypse is successfully introduced with (Subverted), it has been undermined or altered to accomplish a different goal than intended. Much like a monkey's paw, when the Apocalypse finally ends, what the Cult gets is not what was intended; the rules of the game have changed.
An artifact intended to power a Fascist death ray is instead subverted to power a Cult's Dragon-Temple; a Dragon-Temple's Accord against kissing is finally subverted as two athletes passionately embrace in full view of the city. Once (Subverted), an Accord would be unreasonable to reinstate and it would take a miracle to organize that Apocalypse for a second time.
Mechanically, (Subverted) Elements are treated as (Disputed) Elements for the rest of the Narrative. The Directors may consider if it would be appropriate to mitigate (Subverted) for special cases, or if enough time has passed and the Cult which sought the Apocalypse or Accords have a new plan. The new plan should be sufficiently unique from prior attempts!

neverland-system/cues/to-target.html.title: to target
neverland-system/cues/to-target.html.description:
Tenses (v): (past), (present), (progressive) Laconic:To target an Element is
Determining what Targets a Cue actually targets will require critical thinking skills outside the scope of this work. Listen to the Player casting the Cue and determine how the Cue actually plays out before determining what they intended to target; afterwards, make the determination of what they actually targeted at point of Cue resolution.
For example, a Cue might be aimed at an Actor, only for that Actor to duck down and tie their shoes; the Cue might actually splash harmlessly against a wall. On the other hand, a Cue intended to hurt the feelings of an Actor might be audible to a guilty eavesdropper; if they can observe, they may also be a valid target of the Cue! Use your best judgement and talk it over with the Table when in doubt.Mechanically,see also
When an Element has been inflicted with (That) or (Those), it is the Target of the current Cue. When the Cue resolves, it will manipulate those Targets! Additionally, if the Cue has a Feature (such as (That) or (Those) for to target), (Those) Targets will be inflicted with (That) Feature.
Directors should take note that both (That) and (Those) are (Implied) Features, and will not be necessary to denote.
Mechanically, (That) and (Those) both denote the current Targets of a Cue. They are (Implied) Features and typically do not need to be denoted.

neverland-system/cues/to-welcome.html.title: to welcome
neverland-system/cues/to-welcome.html.description:
Tenses (v): welcomed (past), welcome (present), welcoming (progressive) Laconic: To have a Stage personally acknowledge your presence. To welcome an Actor to a Stage is to reveal a number of (Observed) Statements about a Stage to the other Players at the Table. Players tend not to know much, if anything, about the setting in which the story takes place. It falls to the most qualified Player at the Table to describe a Stage, effectively 'grounding' the Table by giving them a good idea of what their Actors are looking at in this very moment.
Typically, welcoming Actors to a Stage should be followed by describing the Stage's Accords to other Players and notifying the Table if there are any governing Dragon-Temple ruling over the area; these are considerations that are important to the mechanics of some Cues and Positions.
If the most qualified Player is ever unsure about whether or not something is (Present) or reasonable, they should defer to the Directors for guidance.see also
When an Actor has been introduced with (Welcomed), they have been accepted by the local Dragon-Temple as a guest or tourist and will be treated as such by local Cults. Cultists will know instinctively that an Actor is an outsider; for an outsider to blend in, they'll need to disguise themselves or participate in Cult activities. Cults are not required to treat their guests well; with that said, any Element participating in Cult activities is far more likely to be welcomed than hunted down and killed. Actors are encouraged to 'play along' and follow the Accords to maintain their (Welcomed) status.

neverland-system/cues/to-wick.html.title: to wick
neverland-system/cues/to-wick.html.description:
Tenses (v): wicked (past), wick (present), wicking (progressive) Laconic: To make something seem more magic than it is.To wick a Statement is to ask for an interpretation of events that is in line with the Lines of a given Narrative. There are, at times, conflicts between two understandings of magic; two Actors may have different views about a given subject, with one insisting their magic functions in a way that would be impossible in the second Actor's version of magic. To resolve this conflict could require Conflict or Arbitration; however, savvy Players may instead choose to introduce a Thread or an Alternative that explains how a Statement works for both Actors, satisfying the madness of both.
Wicking is ultimately temporary; it allows for a Result to factor in two different logical systems without threatening either logical framework. Wicking is the equivalent of claiming that there is a logical explanation while not examining it further than needed for the example. Both parties now have a reasonable Alternative that seems sensible enough for the magic to function. Wicking is also a weakness, however; by wicking a Statement, you introduce the (Wicked) Feature and make it susceptible to melting.see also
When a Statement has been denoted with (Wicked), it's probably been wicked over the course of Conflict. Now that it exists as a bridge between two Lines, it links them together and produces a fragile Statement that is highly susceptible to melting, snuffing, and burning. Care should always be taken to denote a Statement was wicked during Arbitration.

neverland-system/elements/courts/consort.title: Consort
neverland-system/elements/courts/consort.description:
Laconic: Consorts are flexible and may change their roles during Intermissions.
The Consort, is the fifth Noble Court of the Carrow Deck. Consorts share traits in common with the Peasants of the Lower Courts, but are capable of becoming a Witch, Maiden, Nomad or Knight. Consorts are highly valued by Neverlanders, as their latent magical energies make them ideal contractors. Peasants are drawn to their magnetic charisma even before their latent magical energies are awoken; it is rumored that the easiest way to identify a Consort is to look for those who have many admirers.
Consort Pockets may store Props from any Noble Court.
Consort Results are (Vulnerable) to wicking.
Consort Actors rarely remain Consorts once bonded to a Neverlander.
Neverlander Consorts may permanently bond with another Consort.

neverland-system/elements/courts/courts..title: Courts
neverland-system/elements/courts/courts..description:
The Courts are the roles Actors fill in a Narrative. All Actors belong to at least one Court. Courts give Actors additional Cues they may cast.
Peasants and Prins compose the Lower Courts, with each member of the Lower Court able to cast a series of Miracles based on their relationship to the Sephirot of the Narrative Awareness System. This blessing is faith-based, with each Peasant demonstrating a unique connection to their Sephirah.
The Noble Courts are made of the Prin, the Nomad, the Knight, the Maiden and the Witch. Nobles were once Peasants before making a deal with a Neverlander and subsequently learning how to cast magic. Each Noble of the Court has a unique connection to the twenty magical emotions of the Narrative Awareness System, and are able to influence others by relying on those emotions. The full spectrum of Miracles is also available to the Noble Courts, as they are specifically ordained by the Sephirah with godlike abilities.
Neverlanders, as imaginary creatures that live outside of space and time, do not have a single Court; they may choose to assume the role of any of the Lower Courts at the start of an Act and maintain that role until the end of the Act. This willingness to play different roles gives them the full breadth of abilities granted by the Narrative Awareness System to her blessed chosen.

neverland-system/elements/praxis/mechani.title: Mechanics
neverland-system/elements/praxis/mechani.description:
Mechanics are the physical and mechanical forms of Praxis which produce Results from objective inputs. Mechanics are predictable actions, and the same inputs under the same conditions will always produce the same outputs without exception. They are the bedrock of the Neverlands System, in that someone is not playing the Neverlands System if they are not using these Mechanics.
Mechanics are typically prefaced by the word mechanically in English Translations of the Neverlands System. When you see the phrase Mechanically, assume that everything which follows is a mechanic.
Cues can be identified by the presence of an underlined verb, such as to relocate. Cues may come in several verb forms, such as relocated and relocating; all variants should be listed in the Cue page.
Dynamics will always be followed by the suffix -dynamic and will never be plural, as seen in the term Maid-dynamic. If only a Dynamic is listed in text, as in the phrase Knight-dynamic Prop, anything with that Dynamic is a valid Target for the Mechanic.
Features can be identified by their use of parentheses around the Feature, such as in (Pinned) and (Canonized). If it doesn't have parentheses, it's a Cue, not a Feature. If only a Feature is listed in text, as in the phrase (Welcomed) Actor, anything with that Feature is a valid Target for the Mechanic.
Pockets will likely be followed by the suffix -pocket, as seen in the term Witch-pockets. They may have nicknames in addition to their -pocket forms.
Statements may be followed by Value-dynamic Notes such as (Fact), (Fiction), (Opinion) or (Prompt), though only (Fiction) is required due to the Theory of Honesty. Other Value-dynamics may be merely implied as part of the Statement.
Position-dynamic Notes are always implied and should only be denoted as necessary when mechanics require them to be clarified, otherwise your Conflicts will be much slower than intended.
Mechanics have three outcomes depending on their odds of success; Bright, Dim and Dull. We call these outcomes Results, and there are four ways to dispute those Results; melting, wicking, burning and snuffing. Think of a Mechanic like a candle; you can set the candle on fire and it will produce a light. You can let the candle melt and learn what it smells like and what it's made of; you can add fuel to a melted candle by adding a new wick to burn. Or, you could snuff the candle out, reducing its flame to nothing but smoke!
The metaphor of 'Mechanics as candles' is used to make it easier to understand how Mechanics work for new Players. They should not be confused with the candles of a Candelabra, which are Props with the ability to pocket Results; Candelabra follow mechanical rules and are Mechanics, but Mechanics are not Candelabra.
What follows is a non-comprehensive list of Mechanics in the Neverlands System! Scribes have attempted to keep the list accurate and concise for clarity. Mechanics should be accompanied by their laconic forms and Threads leading to their respective articles in alphabetical order. Following the Theory of Clarity, Cues, Dynamics, Features and Songs are all listed on their respective pages, with only top-level categorization methods listed here.

neverland-system/elements/praxis/poems.h.title: Poems
neverland-system/elements/praxis/poems.h.description:
Lore mechanics are optional mechanics that implement the story of the Neverlands; these mechanics follow the journey of the Goddess of Suffering, N'as as she tries and fails to comprehend suffering and empowers Mortals along the way. These rules and mechanics rely on a story influenced entirely by this single figure, and so removal of her figure from your Narratives will require some creative legwork from your Directors to adapt and improvise different parts of the system to account for her absence.
N'as is strictly based on a Deist interpretation of the Jewish pantheon; she should feel familiar to modern scholars of faith because she is the God of the Abrahamic religions. Each of her Sephirot match up to the polytheistic pantheon that made up God in the Jewish faith, and her philosophy is that of a creator-god who has no memories of how she came to create the universe.
While she is a respectful and faithful adaptation of existing religious doctrine, there are problems with her adaptation that may bring Discomfort to Players at your table. For one, the interpretation of N'as used by this book paints a picture of a God who is not all-powerful and all-knowing.N'as has gaps in her skill set that prevent her from being truly alien, and more relatable to humans; she has genders, she identifies by names, she will engage in personal conversation and do menial tasks. She is not regarded as a creative figure, even though she has made all of creation; she has personal flaws and an inhuman attitude towards suffering. This is a different picture of God than the one painted by many modern believers, and though it is accurate to an understanding of the God from the Bible, it conflicts with modern doctrine. Some Players may be discomforted by this notion.
Secondly, N'as is explicitly painted as an antagonistic figure in some mechanics and lore. Her death is the explicit goal of the Council of Knots, and she encourages her children to act against her; this is because N'as believes in free will, even when that free will would turn her children against her. This is consistent with modern doctrine, though it may discomfort Players who want to believe in the existence of Hell as an eternal punishment.Following on that note, the third way N'as may discomfort your Players is that she encourages all religions and occult practices. As part of her commitment to free will, she has allowed every society to develop their own myths and pantheons; ultimately, she does not require the worship of Mortals, and so she has no special jealousy or hatred towards those who practice an improper faith, and even rewards those who worship one another. Many religions believe that to exist in any faith besides the true faith is to condemn oneself to suffer, but N'as is truly agnostic towards other faiths. This interpretation may discomfort some Players at the Table.
N'as is a multifaceted mechanical being and she enables most, if not all stories; if a story would make sense not to include N'as, she's unlikely to make herself known. In the Neverlands, there are many ever-changing Constellations with their own Dragon-Temples and Cults. Each one would feel so different from Earth and have their own Accords; at no point in the process of playing with the Neverlands System should Players feel like her presence is mandatory. So, she is non-obtrusive; more than that, she is useful.
N'as and her Sephirah are easy to use tools to quickly divide Mages into various categories of spellcraft; her various Blessings are made for the convenience of Directors to use as they see fit. She is an Inkwell for a pre-made Cult (the Cult of Suffering) that can be given to Players who are struggling to make their own Cult; she is a plot contrivance that can be used to initiate the Narrative or help it move along. Do not be afraid to use N'as in your stories, but consult with the Table to first determine if she is needed.

neverland-system/elements/praxis/praxis..title: Praxis
neverland-system/elements/praxis/praxis..description:
Disambiguation: Elements, Mechanics, Notes, Theories and Poems are all forms of Praxis; Praxiology (the study of Praxis) also leads here.
Laconic: The actions Players take to play the Neverlands System.
Praxis is the process of changing Narratives to suit the needs of your Tables. Without input from the Players and Directors at the Table, Narratives are dead and lifeless. Narratives require interaction, and Praxis demonstrates the ways in which Players might interact with Narratives and one another.
Every process in the Neverlands System is a form of Praxis:
Elements are the metaphorical and meta-textual forms of Praxis. Elements are the building blocks of a Narrative; its Actors, the Stages they play upon, the Dynamics they share and the Scenes they play in. Each individual component of the Narrative is an Element. Elements are used to represent and reference other concepts in the Narrative for ease of participation.
Mechanics are the physical and mechanical forms of Praxis which produce Results from objective inputs. Mechanics have three outcomes depending on their success; Bright, Dim and Dull. Mechanics are predictable, and the same inputs will always produce the same outputs without exception. They are the bedrock of the Neverlands System and aid in Player understanding.
Notes are the notarial and administrative forms of Praxis. Notes are objective observations made by Tables with the intent to use them as guard rails for Narratives; they are predictable, and can each take the form of Facts, Fictions, Prompts and Opinions. As the bedrock that Narratives are built upon, Notes will always produce the same outputs without exception.
Theories are the social and ethical forms of Praxis. There are many ways to engage with a Narrative, but some forms of engagement are deleterious to social welfare and fun. Scribes encourage Players to read through the Theories behind the Neverlands System to build a mental framework of the Praxis this system embodies. By understanding Theories, one can better execute Praxis.
Poems are the narrative and dramatic forms of Praxis. These are the elements of the Neverlands System which are most open to interpretation, designed to make it easy to produce a Narrative which the Table can enjoy writing together. They are flavorful and full of allusions to literary and world history, but they are subjective; a Poem is easily misunderstood.
Directors and the Table are encouraged to write their own Poetry, especially about their own Actors and Constellations, but there is no shame in using what has also been provided.
Any Praxis could be a hybrid of multiple forms of Praxis; Features are Mechanics because they are used to create predictable Results, but they are also Notes because they could be used in Arbitration.
Laconic: Executing Praxis faithfully is a fool's errand.
Scribes have discouraged Players from thinking of Praxis as a rigid practice. Instead, Players are instead encouraged to consult the Neverlands System as if it were a living document, a creature of paper and text that could be mistaken and may need to be amended in the future. Scribes have tried to make the Theories behind each decision the Neverland System makes as clear as possible, but as Scribes are Mortals they are not omniscient.
Each reader will come away with a different understanding of the Neverlands System, and each understanding will be further and further distorted from the deeply held Opinions of the Scribes; see the Theory of Authorial Intent for more information on this process. With this understanding in mind, the Scribes hope that whenever Players find Theories or Mechanics too burdensome or uncomfortable, they keep in mind the Theory of Fun.Players are discouraged from spending their limited energies in a pursuit that does not bring them joy. With that said, Scribes hope that Players will find the Neverlands System both fun and memorable for years to come!

neverland-system/elements/statements/dyn.title: Dynamics
neverland-system/elements/statements/dyn.description: Dynamics are Features that act as modifiers for Props and Positions. There are two kinds of Dynamics: Major Dynamics which broadly outline a series of Rules that a Prop must follow, and Minor Dynamics which help Players differentiate between different Props and determine that Prop's magical affinity. Both Major Dynamics and Minor Dynamics share a Pocket with their Prop, and sometimes Major Dynamics can define where a Prop can be Pocketed. Players are encouraged to mix and match different Dynamics on the Props in their Pockets to form unique load-outs for their Actors.

neverland-system/elements/statements/res.title: Dim
neverland-system/elements/statements/res.description:
A Dim Result is a Results which indicates both partial failure and partial success. Dim Results occur when an Actor is Risk and Duress or acting without experience, but somehow manage to fumble together a working solution for their problem. Actors can perform better with guidance, and even a Dull Result can become a Dim Result if they use Candles or act in concert with their team. A to snuff could also turn a Bright Result into a Dim Result, as even masters of a craft still make mistakes now and then.
A shortage of vital materials or lacking tools could also influence and worsen the Result of Arbitration; there is such a thing as wrong place and wrong time. Dim Results should not be overwhelmingly negative, but indicate that progress will be slow or that opposing parties will be given more time to respond. In Conflict, a Dim Result could indicate each side of the Conflict is evenly matched; the Actor who earned a Dim Result will need to use more risky Statements to make further progress in that Conflict.

neverland-system/elements/statements/res.title: Dull
neverland-system/elements/statements/res.description:
A Dull Result is a Results which indicates complete or utter failure. Dull Results come about in many ways; an Actor performing techniques outside their realm of expertise is likely to fail, especially if they are performing under duress. Failure can also be caused when a Director plays a to snuff on a Dim Result and the Player does not have Candles in their Pockets.
Failure does not always imply a bad result; there are times in which a failure is preferable or even desirable. Narratives in which the hero always wins and never faces conflict tend to leave their readers drained of energy. Failure can bring risks, and risks draw the Table deeper into the Narratives. Some losses now can also breed success later; never forget that victories are made of more than a single Conflict.
Making a Statement will always carry the risk that your words will be misconstrued or used against you. With that said, it is more fun to make a Statement than to sit back and passively accept the status quo. Fascism is not beaten by sitting back and allowing them to perform evil deeds; a community cannot be built if the community is unwilling to cooperate. Have some fun and gamble! You'll never regret your decisions if you leave an interesting story behind.

neverland-system/elements/statements/res.title: Results
neverland-system/elements/statements/res.description: A Result is both a Rule and a Narrative Element

neverland-system/elements/statements/pos.title: Positions
neverland-system/elements/statements/pos.description:
A Position is a clarifying term for a Statement; they are interchangeable terms. Any Statement can be a Position, and all Positions are Statements.
Positions are Statements that can be held by an Actor; when Conflict begins, every Actor who enjoins themselves to Conflict must hold at least one Position. Positions are both metaphorical and physical; taking a Position implies a physical presence on the Stage and a metaphysical presence in Canon.
Every Position is implied to have at least two Major Dynamics; these Major Dynamics should describe the Rules of the Position, in both a physical and metaphorical sense. It is the task of the Directors to extrapolate the behavior of a Position from its Major Dynamics. Most Position Rules are simple to extrapolate, but examples below can help clarify the concept for readers.
Foundations are likely to serve as firm footholds physically, and unshakable worldviews in a metaphorical sense.
Crossovers are often physical apertures like doorways and windows, but they can also be metaphorical transitions between arguments.
Starters can be stairwells with dozens of landings, but as a metaphor they're great for challenging other Positions.
Dead Ends lead to dead ends, and as a metaphor they are no different.
Threads can act as physical bridges between two places, but they can also serve as a bridge between two disconnected arguments.
Inkwells could be the source of water for a river, or they could be the source of evidence for a litany of metaphorical arguments.
Hills could be mounds of earth like their namesake, or they could be arguments that need to be defended and underpin an Actor's philosophy.
Slopes can be ramps to ease someone down the stairs, or they could be a series of slippery arguments of increasing intensity.
Hazards could be pits of spikes, or they could be the venomous barbs of a cruel and hateful Fascist.
Quagmires could be quicksand, or they could be arguments that are self-defeating in nature.
Alternatives could be firm handholds, or they could be a quick trip into the abyss. It's all about interpretation, from a metaphorical perspective.

neverland-system/elements/statements/sta.title: Statements
neverland-system/elements/statements/sta.description:
Statements are A Fact is a Statement which has been determined by the Directors to be true. Truth as a philosophical concept is not something which can be measured by any mortal rule book, but when the Directors declares a Statement is Fact, then all Players should act as if they knew that Statement to be true beyond a doubt. True Statements are unlikely to be validated as false Statements, only obfuscated with lies and alternatives to the truth of reality.
With that said, things we know to be true about our reality now may not be true forever. If the Director determines that a Statement being treated as Fact detracts from the enjoyment and entertainment of the other Players at the Table they may retroactively change a Statement to be Fiction instead. This is a useful feature of Statements that can avoid many awkward or uncomfortable conversations. No Statement made within the context of the Neverlands System is immutable, and Players should be willing to re-evaluate their prior assumptions to come to new conclusions about reality and see how those new assumptions impact the Narratives.

neverland-system/elements/statements/thr.title: Threads
neverland-system/elements/statements/thr.description:
Laconic: Two or more Statements are linked by a different Statement.A Thread is a Major Dynamic of Positions that signals a link between other adjacent Positions. The following facts about Threads are true:Positions supported by a Thread are logically linked to other Positions.
Threads act as transitions between two Positions. They allow Actors to easily relocate along those Positions while still holding each Position, as the Positions are linked together and support one another. As long as the Thread is not severed, the Positions are functionally identical.
If an Actor holds a Thread, they hold all linked Positions as well.
If a Position is true and valid, and the other Position linked by a Thread is also true and valid, then by holding a Position on the Thread an Actor is necessarily assuming both Positions to be true and valid. Threads may share the Major Dynamics of all linked Positions.
When appropriate, Directors should interpret Threads as linking two Positions together in such a way that a Thread seamlessly blends between two Positions. Threads must compensate for their connections if they do not want to gain the Major Dynamics of those Positions. When appropriate, it is implied that Threads share all Major Dynamics.
Example: A preface such as 'the smart-ass is right, we have to work together' may sufficiently mitigate a harsh Statement by acknowledging that a Position linked to this one is a Hazard, while also implying there is a valid point being made. If the Caster had neglected to call out the other Actor for their rude behavior, their new Thread would be a Hazard as well.
Threads may at times share all other Elements of their linked Positions.
As Threads exist to bridge two concepts together, they will take some of those concepts into themselves through their nature. Actors may freely move, for example, Props between two linked Positions thanks to a Thread ; while that Thread may not contain those Props as Threads lack Pockets unless (Pocketed), it could allow the Actor to carry those Props across.

neverland-system/elements/accords.html.title: Accords
neverland-system/elements/accords.html.description:
Laconic: The physics and laws that society have agreed to enforce.An Accord is a Line concerning the physics of a Dragon-Temple or Constellation. Dragon-Temples have more than just physics of motion; they have laws and they have cultural norms and expectations. Some Dragon-Temples even have their own unique forms of magical thinking; a Cult may believe that magic operates in one way magic from other Cults do not. Accords regulate that magic in plain, easy to decipher terms for Players.
Actors don't usually have access to Accords. To reveal the contents of an Accord, Actors will likely need to go to the source-- a university, a library, the Dragon-Temple's Heart, or speak to one of the higher-ranking Priestesses with knowledge of the Accords. The Table, on the other hand, has full knowledge of the Accords when reasonable; they may ask the Directors if something is against or in line with the Accords, and the Directors must be honest about the content of the Accords where it matters.
Dragon-Temples may only change their Accords during Intermissions, and changing their Accords counts as an Apocalypse. This prevents the Table from being caught off guard by changes to the physics and laws of a region without having any input into how the story proceeds. We strongly recommend that Directors avoid improvising this Rules.

neverland-system/elements/acts.html.title: Acts
neverland-system/elements/acts.html.description: Laconic: A series of scenes with an Apocalyptic conclusion.

neverland-system/elements/assets.html.title: Assets
neverland-system/elements/assets.html.description:
Laconic: The assets and properties managed by a cult.An Asset is a Prop or Stage that has been pocketed by a Cult rather than an individual Actor. Assets that have been pocketed by a Cult may be played by Cultists who are in good standing with the Cult. To maintain good standing, Cultists must be both able and willing to defend the Cult against an Apocalypse, or at the very least a double agent pretending to stand by the Cult until their sudden and inevitable betrayal.
When an Asset is used by a Cultist during an Act, that Asset is unable to be used by any other Cultist that Act; that Cultist may be able to use the Asset multiple times, or it may only have a single use for the entire Act before needing to be refilled. Directors should use common sense judgement to determine how many times an Asset can be used during an Act.
If the Asset is a service another Cultist provides, then a Cultist should be able to receive the services of the other Cultist for a negligible fee. Cultists are only able to provide a single service for a negligible fee over the course of an Act; that service can be comprehensive, elaborate or even holistic

neverland-system/elements/blessings.html.title: Blessings
neverland-system/elements/blessings.html.description: Laconic: The passive benefits of being a favored child of N'as.

neverland-system/elements/casters.html.title: Casters
neverland-system/elements/casters.html.description: Laconic: The Actor casting a Cue.

neverland-system/elements/conflicts.html.title: Conflicts
neverland-system/elements/conflicts.html.description:
A Conflict occurs when an Actor has a need which cannot be met without inconveniencing another Element of the Neverlands System. If the Actor can simply retrieve a Prop from a counter-top, this is not a Conflict; no other Element of the System is inconvenienced by their behavior. Inconveniences must require that one or both parties of a Conflict step aside so that the other can act according to their own wishes.Conflict is only initiated when the inconvenience is irreconcilable; if either party to a Conflict backs off before the Conflict begins, the Conflict does not begin. As for minor disagreements and squabbles between bickering housemates or beleaguered bureaucrats, Conflict may occur so fast and unwittingly that no party even realizes Conflict has occurred. In text, we refer to this as informal conflict, since both parties have resolved their Conflict before Conflict could be formalized. But when is a Conflict formalized?
Formal Conflict begins when the Directors believe it would be more interesting to engage in a protracted discussion or battle with the other Players at the table. Formal Conflict needs stakes, and it requires involved parties to care deeply about the topic at hand. Formal Conflict opens up the Rack for play, and the Directors begin Formal Conflict by following the steps in the Order of Conflict Resolution.
When Formal Conflict is in play, there are Open Conflict States (otherwise known as Open States) and Closed Conflict States (otherwise known as Closed States). Players and Directors should consult the Conflict State Handbook to determine whether Conflict is in a Closed or Open State, but know that the Directors decide if Conflict is in an Open State or not.
During an Open State, any Player at the Table who has enjoined themselves to the Conflict may make Statements and play them to the Rack. Playing a Statement to the Rack is risky since it opens your Actor up to attacks, but rewarding if the Statement makes it to Canon. Among other benefits, once you've made a Statement and it has been canonized, Players can start playing Cues and Strikes to pin the other Actors in the Conflict.During a Closed State, Players should listen closely to the Directors as they narrate what happens. Directors may ask Players to speak during a Closed State, but no Statements may be played to the Rack until Conflict is in an Open State again. Examples of a Closed State include whenever another Player is making a Statement, when a Statement on the top of the Rack is being read into Canon, and when another Player enjoins the Conflict with one of their Actors.Conflict concludes when any party enjoined to the Conflict is unwilling to continue Conflict. At the end of Conflict, each pinned Actor is inflicted with a Condition based on their Position when they were pinned. Actors may have their opinions swayed, be inflicted with injuries, or even mutate and change in magical and unpredictable ways, but all Players involved should consent to the results. If Conflict ended because all Actors sharing a critical Position were pinned, then all of the Actors who shared that critical Position are inflicted with yet another Condition. This Condition must be the same for all Actors, and Directors should select an appropriate Condition.There's nothing stopping a Player or Director from immediately issuing another request for Conflict, especially if they feel like they have the upper hand. Directors are not required to empty the Rack or Canon if there are subsequent Conflicts, but it is a given that the Table may remove old and unrelated Statements from Canon; the previous Conflict is over, after all. The party initiating the subsequent Conflict must claim a new Position, and then if any Actor voices their disagreement with this new Position, those Actor(s) are immediately drawn into a new Conflict.While these rules may look burdensome and confusing on their surface, after a single round of play it should be apparent that Conflict in the Neverlands System should look like something between witty sword banter and a courtroom brawl. The Directors will act as judges, overseeing each move taken by the Players as they attempt to win over against the arguments of fascists, witches and fae terrorists.Those arguments may be sophistry, or they may be fists of flame and fury; whatever the case may be, we recommend Players sharpen their ability to debate by reading their Cues and figuring out new ways to use their abilities. Learn from other Players at the Table, figure out how to disrupt troublesome Statements and look for tricky ways to word your Statements so you don't get caught flat-footed. Think on your feet and you'll be sharp as a tack in no time.

neverland-system/elements/cues.html.title: Cues
neverland-system/elements/cues.html.description:
Laconic: A mechanically significant Statement made with magical thinking.A Cue is a Statement that can be cast by any Mage. Cues are the products of magical thinking; they represent real actions taken in the Mortal Realm by Mortals and have real consequences for the beings around them. A car might be thrown with telekinesis in a Cue, but driven straight into an Actor's body in the real world. Do not consider Cues to be 'fake' just because they are the products of magical thinking.
Cues are mechanical in nature; they are hard mechanical processes with consequences that follow from their resolution. Cues are mechanically firmer than Statements and easier for the Table to track in Conflict, so Players are encouraged to use Cues in Conflict when possible.
Players are also encouraged to look for alternate interpretations of the same Cue; wicking and melting Cues is a great way to poke flaws in opposing arguments and discover new methods to achieve your goals.

neverland-system/elements/cultists.html.title: Cultists
neverland-system/elements/cultists.html.description:
Laconic: Members of a Cult.A Cultist is an Actor who is allied with a Cult. Cultists don't need to agree with every decision a Cult makes; they enjoy the benefits of a Cult by merely agreeing to supply the Cult with some benefit themselves. This benefit can be a resource the Cult needs, a service provided to other Cultists at a steep discount, or labor conducted on behalf of the Cult; benefits provided by a Cultist should be preferential.
Cultists may borrow Assets from a Cult, provided that they are acting in accordance with any Hills the Cult has played to the Outlines this Act. If a Cultist fails to defend a Cult against an Apocalypse

neverland-system/elements/cults.html.title: Cults
neverland-system/elements/cults.html.description: Laconic: A community with a shared history and values.

neverland-system/elements/dances.html.title: Dances
neverland-system/elements/dances.html.description:
Laconic: Overturning the existing order.As every Act of the Narrative opens, every major Cult in the Narrative must play a previously canonized Hill to Canon or a new Hill to the Outlines. After playing their Hill, if they wish, they may plot an Apocalypse. An Apocalypse overturns an existing Hill in Canon or on the Act Rack; if the Apocalypse remains uncontested by any other Cult by the end of the Act, the Hill it targets is permanently (Disputed) and the Cult which played the Apocalypse may canonize a new Hill for their own Cult.The Table always works towards a Revolutionary Apocalypse. The Table inevitably opens with the Revolutionary Apocalypse that establishes the Actors of the Table as a unified team; the first Act of a Narrative will always end with the Table's chosen actors unified towards a single cause, whatever that may be. It is a Fact that there is no force which can prevent the Table from plotting their first Apocalypse. The Table will always know who the heroes of the Narrative are by the end of the first Act and the causes each Actor stands for.
The Table may declare as many Revolutionary Apocalypses as there are Hills to die on; successfully disputing a Hill will let the Table play an additional Hill to the Outlines. Failure to complete any Revolutionary Apocalypse in the Act during which it was declared results in all Hills played to the Rack by the Table being snuffed during the final Arbitration of the Outlines.
Inevitably, the Fascists will attempt to perform an Apocalypse wherever and whenever they wish to hold power. Fascists inevitably open the Narrative with one successful Apocalypse; no matter what the Players do, they will succeed at conquering enough territory to pose a credible threat to the Table as a whole. It is a Fact that the Table will know the identity of the (Figurehead) Fascist by the end of the first Act, and it is a Fact that the Fascists will hold enough power to both threaten and resist the Table by the end of the first Act. This is not to say that the Fascists are competent or even have a coherent plan; rather, their first Apocalypse will always succeed because the Narrative exists in response to the actions of the Fascists.
At the end of each Act in the Narrative, the Fascists will always either attempt to thwart a Revolutionary Apocalypse (if one exists) and start an Apocalypse of their own; even if another Cult starts their own Apocalypse, the Fascists will always take advantage of that Apocalypse to start a different Fascist Apocalypse. If there are multiple Fascist Cults, Directors are encouraged to have all Fascist Cults work together to conduct a single Apocalypse and interfere in every other Apocalypse rather than start multiple Apocalypses.
To challenge an Apocalypse, a Cult must successfully fend off all challengers while maintaining their Hill(s). Whenever a Cultist enters an appropriate Conflict during an Apocalypse, their first Foundation must always be linked to one Hill by a Thread. Cultists must not be relocated from their Hill, pinned against their own Hill, or witness the Thread between the Hill and Foundation become severed during any Conflict relevant to the Apocalypse. If they meet any of these three conditions, that Cultist is pinned for all subsequent Conflicts.
When a sufficient number of Cultists are pinned, such that defending against the Apocalypse is no longer possible, the Director declares that the Apocalypse was successful and introduces (Disputed) on the opposing Hill. The Director may then allow the Cult which successfully completed their Apocalypse to introduce an appropriate Hill to the Act Rack; most often appropriate hills include freshly captured territory, turning a Cultist into a (Supporting) Actor for the Table or swaying the losing Cult to support the winning Cult's cause.
Dissolving a Cult in response to an Apocalypse is difficult as long as they maintain some semblance of power; Directors should exercise caution when deciding if a Cult should dissolve in the wake of an Apocalypse. If it would be more reasonable to dissolve the Cult's Assets and Cultists and reallocate the Cultists among the Fascists, Table and other interested Cults, the Director is encouraged to make those changes.If a Fascist Cult no longer has any useful Hills to die on, then the (Figurehead) Fascist commits to the Last Apocalypse. The Last Apocalypse is a last-ditch effort to hold onto power; Fascists will break any and every principle of their moral code (which is saying a lot, as they have no morals) in order to hold onto power. They will acquire nuclear weapons, threaten to drive their ship into a black hole, infuse themselves with the tainted ichor of gods; they will toss away the entire potential of their movement to conduct one last awful spell to destroy everything.It is a fact that if the Last Apocalypse succeeds, the Narrative is not yet over. There is always a chance to fight back, to try one more time to make the world a better place. There is one more fact to keep in mind; the Last Apocalypse is the moment in which the Fascist Cult is most vulnerable. If the Table can succeed at pushing back the Last Apocalypse, if every Cult can gather their power in one final push, the threat of Fascism can be eradicated and the Players can finally secure their hard-earned victory. Good luck.

neverland-system/elements/dragon-breath..title: Dragon Breath
neverland-system/elements/dragon-breath..description:
The Breath of a Dragon-Temple is the manner in which that Dragon-Temple supports Mortals who live within it. If the Mortals require oxygen to live, a Dragon-Temple's Breath will include oxygen. If they require food and sustenance, Dragon-Temples will endeavor to supply themselves with berries and dew; Mortals who are (Native) to a Dragon-Temple should be able to live off the Breath a Dragon-Temple provides.
Breath could be seen as the Assets of a Dragon-Temple's Cult, with one key distinction; Breath is not simply provided for free. It is earned by Cultists, requiring them to cast Cues to keep themselves nourished; a Mortal must breathe to draw air, but they must also hunt for food or pay their rent. Breath is hoarded and protected by Dragon-Temples, and even the most devout Priestesses must put in reasonable effort to attain Breath between Intermissions. Failure to procure any Breath may inflict new Features!

neverland-system/elements/dragon-heart.h.title: Dragon Heart
neverland-system/elements/dragon-heart.h.description:
Dragon Hearts are the various bureaucratic institutions that exist within a Dragon-Temple. In a forest, these would be the Mother Trees, if not arboreal management services in a nearby Dragon-Temple; in a city, this would be the city hall or the nunnery of the local Priestesses.
Dragon Hearts keep copies of the Accords for new Priestesses to review and modify; they also serve as a seat from which Priestesses can manage the various Mortals that live in their Dragon-Temple. Dragon-Temples who form Marionettes may also choose to live within their Dragon Hearts, since they tend to be the safest Sanctuaries in the Dragon-Temple.
Only an Apocalypse can subvert the defenses of a Dragon Heart and lay a hand on the Accords of the Dragon-Temple. With that said, losing control of a Dragon Heart is a very real risk Dragon-Temples face; if their Dragon Heart should fall into the hands of their enemies, those foes may freely alter their Accords into unfavorable conditions for the Dragon-Temple. Cities are known to plunder the Hearts of the surrounding wilderness, taking over and depriving other Dragon-Temples of their ability to self-govern.

neverland-system/elements/dragon-scales..title: Dragon Scales
neverland-system/elements/dragon-scales..description:
Dragon Scales are any Stages or Positions that defend or sever a Dragon-Temple from other Dragon-Temples in that Constellation. Scales can be natural boundaries, such as cliff-sides, rivers and mountain-sides. They can also be artificial boundaries; a stone wall, a wooden bridge or even another subordinate Dragon-Temple.
Scales often act as risky Crossovers requiring Actors who wish to pass to complete some rite or ritual. Some Dragon-Temples have permeable boundaries that any Mortals can cross, like an open field with a simple sign at the city limits. Other Dragon-Temples choose to relocate the flow of traffic into predictable passages; that way they can charge tolls, verify identities and deter invasions.
Dragon-Temples use their Scales to keep Breath from fleeing their sphere of influence; a competently managed Dragon-Temple may have a unique ecosystem, and their boundaries will be defined by the edges at which their Breath ends and another Dragon-Temple's Breath begins.
Scales may only be breached or subverted with an Apocalypse, allowing the Table time to respond to any attempt to destroy or reshape a Scale. This Apocalypse must be initiated by a Priestess, either of this Dragon-Temple or an another (Adjacent) Dragon-Temple or Constellation.

neverland-system/elements/dragon-temples.title: Dragon-Temples
neverland-system/elements/dragon-temples.description:
A Dragon-Temple is a Stage and an Actor, a Court and a Divine. It is all of these things, because a Dragon-Temple is a city, a settlement, a region of the world; it is the local land god and it is the people that tend to it. Dragon-Temples can be seen as great concepts Cults are built around; a Dragon-Temple is usually founded or named by someone considered to be important at that time, then over the generations becomes a well-known feature of that Constellation.
Settlements and Sanctuaries are almost all Dragon-Temples; they have a name, Mortals live inside them and people consider themselves to be part of that Dragon-Temple. A Chicagoan lives in Chicago, and Chicago is a living, breathing thing; a squirrel considers itself to belong to the forest it was born in, and the fish considers every river and sea its home. Even an office worker may consider their workplace to be a Dragon-Temple! Each of these places is represented by a Dragon-Temple of varying strength and power.Dragon-Temples are only eliminated when the last drop of water is removed from their river, leaving the skeleton of a creek behind. Dragon-Temples may have their boundaries shift, or bend, or even break in times of war, but they will always adapt to their new shapes for as long as people believe in their existence. Dragon-Temples often bend with the river, they do not fight the way the channels twist and turn.
A Dragon-Temple has four components: an Accords, the Dragon Breath, the Dragon Scales and the Dragon Heart. The Accords define the physics and cultural expectations of a Dragon-Temple; the Breaths are all the things inside a Dragon-Temple that help them support life. The Scales of a Dragon-Temple are their boundaries and walls which protect the life within, and the Hearts of a Dragon-Temple update Accords and manage the Mortals within. Hearts are staffed by Priestesses, Mages and Divines who have vowed to serve the Dragon-Temple to the best of their ability.
To win a game of the Neverlands System, the Table must create and nurture a Dragon-Temple to maturity. One of the Actors of the Players may have to become a Dragon-Temple and play out their Cues; the Directors are welcome to play this role instead if they see it would bring the table discomfort.

neverland-system/elements/fascists.html.title: Fascists
neverland-system/elements/fascists.html.description: Laconic: A morally degenerated creature, typically a Mortal.A Fascist is the result of moral decay over an extended period of time. As an Actor thinks less of other Actors and treats them like mere Props to be used and discarded, they commit a cardinal sin. The Fascist will soon forget those feelings they once cherished; compassion, imagination, reason and joy. They reduce the people around them to mere categories, boxes they can fit all their negative preconceptions inside.There is no kindness hidden deep within the Fascist, and they believe in nothing; they have become devils, great works of evil corrupted by their own hatred. They imitate human speech, pretending to be cognizant of the world, but are dead and hollow on the inside. To rescue a Fascist is to free them from their own ideology; they cling so strongly to their worldview that in some moments, the only form of freedom they can experience is death.Fascists are not to be taken lightly. They, like any other Actor, are free to make decisions. Free will can be dangerous in their hands; Fascists spread their ideology to unwary victims, poisoning them with a toxin that eats away at their soul. The weak succumb; they become (Craven), then (Belligerent). Their souls are chipped away, until they become (Depraved) or worse... (Dreadful). The strong undo the shackles the words of the Fascist place on their heart; they resist that primal urge to succumb to the strong, to kill and to take what is not theirs. The lucky few develop a moral backbone, an immunity to the Fascist curse and a true immunity to the lies they spread.Fascists have lost their names to their dark affliction. They are known simply by their title and a letter; a Fascist doctor might be known by Dr. Q, where a Fascist fae might be known as L the Conqueror. Only by freeing themselves of the shackles of Fascism can a Fascist reclaim their name.Players are prohibited from playing Fascists. Actors in play may be former Fascists, but they must not carry the disease of Fascism, as becoming a Fascist is one of few loss conditions in the game. An Actor cannot succumb to Fascism unless their Player is willing to cease playing them until their Fascism is cured.Directors may play Fascists, but only in the abstract; a Fascist is an obstacle to be employed only as an opposing narrative force to the Players. Players may attempt to make deals with Fascists; Fascists will always break their word at their soonest convenience. Players may attempt to reform Fascists; one look away and the Fascist may vanish, to return to their people. Players may attempt to confront Fascists directly... but the Fascist is willing to kill and only looking for an opportunity to do so without reprisal.(Militarized) Fascists are almost impossible to overwhelm with Strikes, Shots and Spells. (Militarized) Fascists Snuff all Cues which target them, should they outnumber the Players twenty to one. It does not matter if they are grouped up or spread out throughout a building; a (Militarized) Fascist compound cannot be taken down directly.That does not mean there are no means to take down Fascists; Fascists traveling in patrols are possible to successfully ambush, even if they wield dangerous weaponry. Siege tactics show some effectiveness, as would leading a force large enough to even out the (Militarized) Fascist's numerical advantages; they are only immune to targeted Cues if they outnumber their foes twenty men to one divine. Twenty men to one divine and a dog? They may quake in their boots.The exception to the rule, (Figurehead) Fascists may be known by their full name and their title, eg. President Armand. Figurehead Fascists represent the final obstacle Players must overcome in order to win. Figurehead Fascists can seamlessly employ the abilities of all other Fascists, as well as the magical abilities of Mages. They have strength rivaling the Divines; simply ambushing a (Figurehead) Fascist will not be enough to defeat them. (Figurehead) Fascists are ultimately replaceable; if they are assassinated in the quiet of night, a new (Figurehead) Fascist will rise to replace them. To win, their ideology must be well and truly refuted in an open forum, visible to the public and unequivocally a victory.
