The exact details of how the final Conflict might play out are provided on the Dances page.

neverland-system/elements/intermissions..title: Intermissions
neverland-system/elements/intermissions..description:
Laconic: Time spent relaxing between risky activities.Intermissions are time spent between stressful and risky activities. Intermissions are important; Mortals need Intermissions to slumber, Mages need Intermissions to unravel, Divines need Intermissions to manage their Cults and Neverlanders need Intermissions to adopt new Courts.
Intermissions may be performed within any Sanctuary, so the rules of Sanctuaries still apply; enjoining Conflict is incredibly difficult to cast during Intermissions. If tensions are high for whatever reason, the Directors should encourage Players to have their Actors bottle up their feelings, take whatever actions they need to reluctantly push through that Intermission and emotionally unload on each other when the next Conflict comes along.
During Intermissions, Cults may also manage their Assets and the Table may exchange Props and Dynamics that are sitting inside their Pockets; any Props or Dynamics not contained within a Pocket can be deposited with the other Assets belonging to the Cult or inside their Sanctuary, where they will likely remain safe and untouched by other parties.
To disrupt the Intermission of a Cult (or to pocket their Assets and forcibly relocate into their Sanctuary, for that matter) is to plan an Apocalypse. The moment that the Apocalypse starts, the Intermission ends abruptly and most benefits of Intermissions are lost, depending on what the Directors find reasonable. The Table should be informed in advance that their Intermission will be interrupted so they may play accordingly.

neverland-system/elements/mages.html.title: Mages
neverland-system/elements/mages.html.description:
Laconic: Mortals with strong emotions and magic.Mortals are fragile things; they live and wallow in suffering their whole lives. Some accept their lots in life; they are sadists who inflict suffering on others, or cogs to turn the wheel of suffering. These Mortals do not appreciate life; they cannot experience artistic beauty because they do not understand what it means to feel pain and grief. They look at their world as a commodity, not an ecosystem of which they are but one part.
A few Mortals cannot handle the pressures of modern society, and they simply break. These are the Mages; the ones who bear suffering on their shoulders, the weight enough to snap their spine. Mages are worn down enough their mind opens up to the possibility of magic, as they have been forcibly torn from all other avenues of escape. They embrace fantasy as a way to handle their crippling suffering, and they instinctively wick reality.
A Mage can intentionally cast Cues. This might seem obvious, but Mortals are not able to cast Cues on their own. From the Mortal's perspective, they are simply living their lives without knowledge of the Rack and Canon; but from a Mage's perspective, each action they take is deliberate, a spell that is cast through complex rites and rituals. Each conversation is an opportunity to make Statements, each Conflict possible to resolve through lateral thinking and deep understanding of Alternatives. The world a Mage observes is not the world a Mortal sees, and Mages are unable to close their eyes once they have been opened.
Mages will naturally come to understand their Court and Suit as they become more familiar with the ways of magic. Most Mages are content once they've learned their Blessing; that connection to a higher being can soothe the soul of a Mage enough to ease their pain. The understanding that life will continue after death, that there is a Goddess who watches over them; that's enough for many. Their understanding of God will vary from Mage to Mage, but their Blessings are

neverland-system/elements/miracles.html.title: Miracles
neverland-system/elements/miracles.html.description: Laconic: Divinely inspired Cues cast by N'as.

neverland-system/elements/pockets.html.title: Pockets
neverland-system/elements/pockets.html.description:
Laconic: Elements are organized into Pockets for storage and play.Pockets are mechanical Elements that represent empty spaces and ambiguous Elements. Any Element or Statement could fit into a Pocket that was of an appropriate shape and size; Costumes could fit into closets, and Typewriters could fit into a violin case. Cuffs might be stored in dresser drawers, and there would be no problem fitting Tomes inside of backpacks! But you could also take a Tome out of your bag and put Spools into your bag instead; we could hardly call the contents of the bag fixed.
Ambiguous Props like the ones found in a bag could be anything, so the space an ambiguous Prop could occupy is called a Pocket! To clarify that ambiguity, Notes denoting what the Pocket contains should be present on any given Pocket. When an Actor pockets a Prop, they are mechanically denoting the contents of the Pocket using the Elements they are pocketing as Notes. For more information, consult the Cue to pocket.
Empty spaces built to play Statements to can also be Pockets. There could theoretically be a Statement inside a Tome; you wouldn't know if the Tome was blank or not until you opened the Tome to read its contents. Since the Tome could contain Statements, if you opened it and found nothing, you could write a Statement in that Tome; likewise, with a writing instrument like an eraser you could wipe away and existing Statement and replace it with your own. This empty space is an ambiguous space, and is therefore a Pocket! For more information on this interaction, consult the Theory of Ambiguity.

neverland-system/elements/statuses.html.title: Statuses
neverland-system/elements/statuses.html.description: Laconic: The exact state of the game at any point in time.

neverland-system/elements/strikes.html.title: Strikes
neverland-system/elements/strikes.html.description: Laconic: Cues with a strong potential to inflict harm.

neverland-system/roles/nomads-of-mystery.title: Nomads of Mystery
neverland-system/roles/nomads-of-mystery.description:
Nomads of Mystery are sometimes known as Depthseekers; they delve deeply into the mysteries of the world, driven by a desire to know more.To cast Depthseeker magic is also known as seeking; when an Element has been influenced by gleam, Mages assume that the Element is found.
Gleam is a potent Emotion; those who feel Gleam are firm, sympathetic, self-sure, sociable, manic, awestruck and depressed.
Depthseekers are born under the Constellation of Mythos and will find themselves welcome in the Constellation of the same name, Carrowthyme.
The Nomad of Mystery burns with passion and zeal for their studies. The Suit of Mystery and the Values of Prompts explain the Nomad's Blessing; the Nomad is capable of asking the right questions at the right time. Their Prompts are canonized when played, burning the Results of all relevant Arbitration.
Nomads of Mystery have additional influence over Dynamics, Props and Features. They may manipulate these Elements with ease, introducing new Features and Dynamics to Props during Conflict rather than spending precious Intermissions. For example, Nomads of Mystery are sometimes known to speak with and manifest their Props as protective guardian spirits.

neverland-system/roles/peasants-of-creat.title: Peasants of Creation
neverland-system/roles/peasants-of-creat.description:
Peasants of the Suit of Creation are Mortals strongly influenced by the Goddess of Suffering, N'as. Beloved children of the Goddess of Suffering and cared for under her embrace, each Peasant has manifested one of her Blessings; these Blessings reflect how that Peasant reflects the nature and beauty of N'as and her Sephirot.
Consorts of Creation - see Consort of Creation.
Second Creative - Creatives of Diresk may always know a way forward, imbued with a supernatural confidence in their sense of direction.
Third Creative - Creatives of Yedrin may find inspiration in nature, able to focus in on natural phenomena to make crystal clear observations.
Fourth Creative - Creatives of Seprix are able to find opportunities anywhere they look, even in the midst of danger and disaster.
Fifth Creative - Creatives of Tracta can leverage their talents to open doors closed to others, using their art as a means to an end.
Sixth Creative - Creatives of Remora are able to vividly impress their own stories upon readers, and their words linger in the ears of their listeners.
Seventh Creative - Creatives of Netrim are powerful wheelers and dealers, quick to make friends and quick to earn confidence.
Eighth Creative - Creatives of Bassur are focused on social change, able to identify and take in the viewpoints of other communities.
Ninth Creative - Creatives of Mysten are able to make moving works of art that connect with others on a fundamental level.
Tenth Creative - Creatives of Dracoa are opportunities in and of themselves, providing work and resources to other Actors.

neverland-system/theories/community-stan.title: Community Standards
neverland-system/theories/community-stan.description: Laconic: The few things that can get your free licenses taken away.Generally speaking, our Attributions and Licenses policy is very generous. All of the Neverlands System is available for personal and commercial use with no need for kickbacks, royalties or creative oversight. We want people to use this project and want to give them the tools to be empowered to create new and interesting things. We also recognize, however, that Fascists enjoy finding wholesome and creative projects and subverting them, and that we need some sort of protection against political campaigns using our project to promote war, child rape and other uncomfortable topics.So, we're spelling it out here; these are the things you're not allowed to do with our System, even given the permissive licenses. By using one of our licenses, you open yourself up to legal liability if you attempt to do one of the following with our system: The use of the Neverland System to advocate for warfare, state-sponsored violence, state-sponsored terrorism or genocide. The use of the Neverland System to fuel and produce works for the purpose of depriving creatives and artists of their livelihood, eg. AI, Pinkerton, and CIA-funded death squads. The use of the Neverland System to produce works designed to maximize harm against a vulnerable group that lacks protections, eg. the production of CSAM and animal abuse pornography. The use of the Neverland System to promote something which is outwardly Fascist or designed to stoke the flames Fascist sentiment in its viewers. This includes works which are designed for the purpose of promoting Fascism but not works which incidentally support Fascist characters or ideas, which would be hard to monitor and dispute and therefore not worth our time. Any work which threatens all of human life, whether it be through climate catastrophe or nuclear holocaust. Honestly, if you try to do some of these and you're somehow protected from legal liability through arcane loopholes, some of us are going to find a way to kill you. That's not a threat, that's a promise. It will be brutal, it will probably be televised and at least one of us will happily martyr themselves if it means protecting human lives and her hard work from your Fascist ideals. So, fuck off, Fascists, and find some other foxhole to squander your worthless lives on.

neverland-system/theories/discomfort.htm.title: Discomfort
neverland-system/theories/discomfort.htm.description:
Disambiguation: Both consent and respect also link to this article. Please read carefully. This article discusses serious and uncomfortable topics potentially inappropriate for readers under the age of 18. We are not shy about the language used in this article. Laconic: Players should be able to consent and refuse to participate in uncomfortable scenes.
Discomfort is a Results that occurs when a Cue or Statement could cause a Player to be too uncomfortable to continue. There are many ways a Player could become uncomfortable; excessively violent, gross or sexual content is likely to start ringing alarm bells. There's nothing inherently wrong with a Narrative containing upsetting material; life is complicated, and people are capable of extremely weird or even terrible things if left to their own devices. With that said, a Player should not be asked to play through an uncomfortable scene just because it 'makes sense' in Canon. Players should always have the ability to consent.How can Directors ensure Players will consent to a Scene containing objectionable materials? Firstly, Directors should be willing to tone down the content in a Scene if the Table expresses discomfort. Subjects such as rape, genocide, war, murder, racism, sexism and homophobia are likely to cause Players to pause. Some of them have lived experiences which echo these experiences; one in three women have experienced a non-consensual sexual encounter, often with someone they trusted or a family friend. Asking a Player who has been taken advantage of to make a deal with an Actor who traffics women for sex is, to put it lightly, inconsiderate.With that said, Directors need to grasp the boundaries of themselves and their Players without flatly asking if they've been abused before; the goal of a Director is not to dredge through uncomfortable memories, but to respect their Players preemptively. We recommend that, during the first session and at least once a year, Directors sit the Table down and learn what makes the Players uncomfortable. The Director should voice their own boundaries, and invite the rest of the Players to do the same.Write down the strictest boundaries in each category; sexual content, violent content, obscene content and hate speech. Then, simply avoid mentioning those subjects in Narratives in future play. With that said, avoiding triggers and uncomfortable scenes is easier said than done.
Players are fighting against Fascists, and Fascists have done every uncomfortable thing in the book. In the Zoot Suit Riots of the 1940s, Fascists stripped children naked in the street and savaged them. In World War II, Fascists committed the systematic, race-motivated rape, murder and genocide of millions. In modern America, Fascists have successfully perpetuated a culture of racism, sex trafficking and pedophilia and intend to deport and torture millions of Americans for their own sadistic pleasure. Life is uncomfortable, and Fascists need to be dealt with to create a better world; Fascists are uncomfortable to confront, and they do not hide their inherent evil.How can we talk about the evil of the Fascist without espousing their terrible ideas? How can Directors avoid verbalizing the vile dreams of the Fascist and making the Table uncomfortable?For each category, we've described some potential ways to talk about uncomfortable material in an appropriate manner for a group that is uncomfortable with that subject matter. Should content matter that the Table has already prohibited need to be discussed, hopefully these guidelines will enable a mature (and potentially age-appropriate) conversation with Players. These are, in part, exercises for the reader; once you've grasped one technique for discussing serious topics without endorsing them or causing discomfort, you should be able to apply those same methods in different ways with other uncomfortable topics. Above all else, respect the boundaries of the Table. Ensure that they are actually comfortable with the subject matter being discussed in broad terms; if they are not, do not engage with these techniques either.Starting with the easiest of the four categories to break down, we'll take a look at violence and violent content. This can include, but is not limited to, assault, murder, hostage-taking, threats of violence, gore, cannibalism, genocide and other shows of force. Violence is present in most media; children are acclimated with violence from a young age, though the violence they see lacks the blood and gore of adult horror films. Heroes wield swords and slash through their enemies in often bloodless combat, and as we age we are desensitized against that violence.Violence is common, and that is why it will be rare to see a Table which sees simple violence as a boundary. Most Tables are fine, fundamentally, with the idea of death, of violence and gun-play. They will not be squeamish about the very concept of killing goblins and ghouls, but they will be particular about how brutal those deaths are allowed to be. Understanding where their tolerance for bloodshed and gore lies should be the goal when discussing violent content at your Table.For children, try to offer age appropriate forms of violence. Children are unlikely to be exposed to gore and other gruesome details; with that said, you don't need to shy away from the substance of violence itself. Children will accept that there is a hungry, man-eating monster and will not question the logistics of cannibalism; they will accept someone can be shot with a gun and die. Children are not afraid of violence in the ways adults are afraid of violence; they believe themselves to be invincible, that their heroes will never come to serious harm. Keeping their injuries to no more than a bruise or some cuts helps children protect their innocent worldview, though children could no doubt be cajoled into losing an arm or a leg as long as you offered them a cool prosthetic afterwards. They are, in many ways, the easiest Players to please.When it comes to adults, many of us have experienced violence, both as victim and as perpetrator. Some have abusive relationships with their parents, some have been violently mugged and deprived of their belongings; other times, adults have been hit by cars, or taken into custody. A good number of adults are veterans, soldiers who fought and who killed-- who watched their friends and comrades die. For an adult, violence is a very real, very personal part of their life. Understanding your Table means recognizing when a violent scene is likely hitting close to home and pulling away from the Scene to speak to the Table as a group.Simply choosing not to describe events as being violent, gory and nasty is the easiest way to avoid upsetting the Table. Instead of taking detailed descriptions of anatomy as it twists and deforms under pressure, using flowery poetry can accomplish the same goals. Consider substituting a phrase such as 'blown off their head, skullcap and grey matter splashing against the carpet' with another phrase such as 'the shot landed and a crimson flower bloomed above their neck.' Better yet, keeping the description short and sweet can avoid making a Player squeamish.If their Table is more comfortable with violence, Directors should encourage players to be more creative and take liberties with their violent actions. There is comedy inherent to violence; slapstick is known for its wacky sense of humor. Violence in a game does not leave any Player physically harmed in the real world. The only harm an Actor can inflict on a Player is emotional, and violence can be used for drama and tension as well. Consider trying to keep the violence just shy of the edge of what is comfortable for your Table without pushing things too far.Sometimes, however, what a victim wants is the ability to exert power over a situation in which they felt powerless before. Remember that adults are familiar with violence; they were likely left helpless in a violent situation before now, and for them, the ability to exact a 'just' revenge helps them to come to terms with their lived experiences. It is important to allow Players to express themselves and their need for control while also keeping a rein on their behaviors; if the Director feels like a Player is being too violent for the table, or a Player feels uncomfortable with the amount of violence expressed by another Player, they should be encouraged to vocalize those feelings. Bottling up emotions is not healthy for the long-term mental health of the Table and nobody should be made to feel uncomfortable because another Player wants to exercise control over their own destiny.Moving on to the next of the four categories to discuss, sex and sexual topics! The Neverlands System will rarely shy away from sexual topics in the rulebook itself. Sex is human, and the Fascist is the one who seeks to control sex. They are not shy about their predilections; they see sex as a way to control women, to radicalize men and to ostracize the non-binary. While this book is intended for mature audiences, it is also important to realize that not all Players are mature adults and not all mature adults want to discuss sex in their games. So, how can we approach sex and sexuality without making others uncomfortable?First, let's handle children. Children do not understand sex and should not be exposed to it at their young age. Children are largely aromantic as well; they understand the concept of romance well enough, especially if they're exposed to examples of romance when young. Increasingly more often than in previous generations, children are more familiar with the concept of polycules, gay relationships, cheating spouses, intercultural families and pregnancy. It is important to strike a line between educative and tasteful when discussing sexual or romantic topics around children.Avoiding simple mistakes, like making sexual innuendos, are easy enough. Children may make silly requests, however, like wanting their Actor to flirt with another Actor. It is important to recognize these requests for what they are-- a desire to engage with the story and give their Actor greater purpose, not a genuine attempt to flirt. For children, romance is witty banter, people who fight and later get along, and in the ending scene they get married and live happily ever after. Teenagers might have a slightly more nuanced view, but don't mistake their confidence for understanding; teenagers are just as useless and confused as any other kid when it comes to sexual topics, and don't let them convince you otherwise.Children do not want dates (and it would be inappropriate to offer them), they do not want to know about sexual organs and they are definitely not prepared to hear that Fascists are pedophiles. Children are not straight, they're not gay, they don't even known what their own gender is and they have no conception of romance; unless you are a qualified adult in a learning environment, this game should not be used as their first exposure to sex and sexual topics. Respect the intelligence of a child and don't hide the rough details from them; they know what a gay marriage looks like and they won't be impressed if you dance around the subject. But understand their needs are fundamentally different from the needs of adults.When dealing with adults and talking about sexual content, you need to establish very quickly with the Table what kind of Narrative you are all attempting to write. If the goal is to make a polycule of useless twenty-something divines and fairies struggling with society, then it stands to reason that sex and discussion of sex is on the table. That means that, as a fundamental part of the Narrative, Players will be writing Actors who are flirting with other Actors played by other Players. It's the role-playing equivalent of an indirect kiss, and an inexperienced Director can easily see the romance between characters spiral out of control and derail the session.Grasp very firmly why and how your players want to engage with sexual content; if for any reason a member of the Table isn't comfortable being voyeur to weird and obscene gestures, respect their wishes. Consent is important, and there's nothing stopping two Players from having a discussion outside of the Table with more lurid or obscene content. Other times, Players might be fine with the half-naked bard who flirts with every monster they meet-- but they don't want to see those Actors engage in sex while they're forced to watch in the Scene. For the purpose of avoiding discomfort, a graceful 'fade to black' or other poetic metaphors are more appropriate. It is more evocative and interesting to see two characters leave a tent in a state of partial undress; with more left to the imagination, the Table will have plenty to banter about. Light touches, like a kiss on the cheek or hands held close together, are unlikely to cause anyone but the most prudish discomfort. Professions of love for one another can hold more power than any other Statement in play. Avoiding sexual topics is not always exclusive with a lack of romance!But there are other, darker sexual topics which lack romance. Rape, sex trafficking, bestiality, pedophilia and other forms of non-consensual sex are real topics worthy of discussion for inclusion. These are sexual acts between two parties in which one party does not have a say; consent is the key phrase missing from these encounters. So, how can we discuss these four sub-topics of non-consensual sex without making players uncomfortable?When we're talking about bestiality, we're not talking about sleeping with a talking donkey or giant telepathic ant, but a creature which is incapable of giving an emphatic 'yes' and intellectually challenged when compared to an adult mortal. Animals are incapable of granting consent and as a general rule, the system suggests avoiding their inclusion altogether. If there is an animal, consider replacing it with a fae; an imaginary creature that can take many forms, in the same way that mythological gods are often written as taking the shape of an animal to romance mortals. This avoids any possibility of bestiality altogether by removing the 'beast.'When considering how to discuss sex trafficking, consider making it something that Players can do something about. A prostitute kept by his pimp under false pretenses is akin to a princess awaiting rescue; he would very much appreciate his passport back from the pimp, and it is an opportunity for Players to express power over the situation. Be understanding of the inability of those trafficked to fight back against their oppressors. Be cognizant that the ones who are trafficked into sex slavery are just unlucky; anyone could have had the misfortune of being the victim, and it was not their fault for landing in this situation. Some prostitutes may find empowerment through sex work, but these victims are not those fortunate prostitutes. They are people, likely kidnapped as children or imprisoned by criminals, forced to sell their body from an unpleasant age.It does need to be said that an estimated eight million children disappear worldwide every year. This is the result of pedophilia, the third uncomfortable topic that may need to be discussed. It's clear that the Fascist wants to fuck children and that they should not be allowed within nine counties of a child. Their policies overwhelmingly attempt to structure society so they can fuck children; many states allow child marriage, adults work tirelessly to prevent children from receiving sexual education once they reach maturity, and the media children consume teaches sex in a very unhealthy way.Fascists do not desire children because they want to have sex; they want to fuck children to feel powerful. We don't imagine that Directors will particularly want to vocalize these thoughts, especially out loud; while the section on Discussing Hate Speech in this article goes more into depth about how to phrase these thoughts without endorsing them, our recommendation when talking about this topic is simple:Provide two methods for Players. They may imprison the Fascist, or they may kill the Fascist.There's no need to offer other alternatives. By the time a Fascist has started to espouse the ideals of the pedophile, they're long gone. While the long-term project of Fascism necessitates that they fuck and murder children in their craven pursuit for power, most Fascists know this to be intolerable. They don't want to believe their movement is capable of such evils. They genuinely imagine their movement to be for a greater good; that is where the Fascist is wrong. Fascism is a movement of power, and children are the weakest of all of us. They must be put in line, the Fascist reasons, and so it is that they exercise their sexual power over children.Exercising sexual power without consent is rape. As mentioned previously in this article, one in three women have reported being raped; for both men and women this figure is likely to be much higher than reported. This is a topic that we would suggest Directors avoid even if their group is fine with it; even characters who act like creeps should be handled very carefully. Rape is, unfortunately, an epidemic in our society and we have a culture which encourages rape. Consent is considered secondary to the exercise of power, and the Fascist has no qualms about using this power to hurt others. Avoiding this topic altogether is the only way to be sure that nobody is made uncomfortable, because it is very likely that two members of a five-Player Table have already experienced this event in their own life.As a final word, consent is extremely important to consider when discussing sexual topics. Always make sure Players are asking for consent from one another before doing anything sexual or taking away the bodily autonomy of another character.You'll know it when you see it; obscene content is the gross and the puerile. Coarse language, sickness and disease, gore and rot. Mortals are gross, and they probably have cooties; fae aren't much better, since nobody's taught them manners. At some point, sitting the Table down to discuss their boundaries when it comes to obscene content will matter for comfort.Children are familiar with gross and obscene content; they've spent an inordinate amount of their life without manners or insecurity. Children will smear snot on their shirts, leave their room a mess and say neither 'please' nor 'thank you' when you gift them socks for Christmas, but that doesn't mean they enjoy filth. Children will outwardly express discomfort when the Table is too gross for them, and Directors at the Table should listen. For a child, they likely haven't grown up enough to hide their disgust behind nice language, and it's important to not take that feedback personally; listen to their tone and watch their facial expressions.Children know obscenity when they see it, but to be safe it's best to avoid some forms of obscenity around children-- unless you want them growing up with weird fetishes when they grow older, you've got to keep things vanilla. Avoid Actor nudity, since it asks questions you shouldn't have to answer; keep swears appropriate around children. They don't have a filter on their mouth in the same way adults do, so they'll repeat swears freely and without inhibitions. Children lack exposure to violence, and gore may frighten or traumatize them, so avoid those sorts of obscenity as well as anything else too violent for a child.If children are so vulnerable, what is appropriate for them? It's a tough call. Children can comprehend obscenity and will likely find it humorous, but we don't endorse rushing their development. Tasteful obscenity can earn you a laugh; knowing what swears won't get them in trouble at school can also keep them from detention. Try to avoid any obscenity you couldn't see when putting on a cartoon for them to watch.Teenagers are likely to be even more lenient than most children; they expect harder language, alcohol and drugs. They might see some forms of gore as more 'mature' and appropriate for their age; think about classic slasher movies, and the kinds of content that could be seen in your typical PG-13 film.It's also important to recognize when the adults at the Table are grossed out; crawling through a sewer system to reach a bandit hideout sounds well and good on paper, but if your Table is especially squeamish you might want to keep descriptions to a minimum. There's no need to include naked bonfires with satyrs if Players would rather they keep their loincloths worn. Obscenity should be taken on a case by case basis; listen to Players when they vocalize their thoughts, and vocalize your discomfort when you find a Scene too gross to play.Lastly, the Players are fighting Fascists. This is likely to be the hardest role for a Director to embody; they need to vocalize what Fascists believe without endorsing those thoughts. The Fascist is wrong; they believe in hate and dominance over others, and their thought processes are cartoonishly evil. Most mortals have forgotten the cruelty of mankind, but the Neverlands System has not forgotten the horrible thoughts they deemed fit to vocalize.When writing a Fascist, keep in mind that they only mask their speech when they feel it is unsafe to vocalize their thoughts. In public, they will call themselves apolitical and a moderate; they will claim that 'everyone' agrees with them, that 'thugs' make them uncomfortable and talk about the 'bad part of town' like they weren't picking a minority out of a lineup. When a Fascist does not believe they need to hide, they will let loose their real thoughts. They will tell that they think each and every minority is having kids and doing fraud, that they can't wait to start up new concentration camps for homeless queers, about how excited they are to wed and rape a child slave from another country. Fascists are monsters and anyone who can be saved was not yet fully part of their ideological framework.It is important to portray Fascists as the monsters they are. Avoiding the language they use gives them legitimacy, in the same way that the media gives Fascists legitimacy by exclaiming they have legitimate and reasonable views. This is a game about dismantling the power of the Fascist, but expressing their ideals leaves a bad taste in the mouth of the one giving them a voice. Directors shouldn't feel like they have to say the words of the Fascist out loud. Here are a few strategies for talking about Fascists and their beliefs without saying those beliefs outright:For the (Cowardly) Fascist, mask their speech. (Cowardly) Fascists say they believe that their opinions are widely held, but deep down they know how wretched their opinions truly are; they fear that they are consumed by evil thoughts, and so they speak carefully. (Cowardly) Fascists could claim to be moderate or apolitical; they could make frequent reference to 'thinking what we're all thinking.' They'll not say anything outwardly sexist or racist; they are cowards, after all. Perhaps they'll use coded language, like calling a minority population 'thugs' or a queer subculture 'against god.' They grumble about taxes and call themselves 'fiscally conservative.'(Cowardly) Fascists will dehumanize the communities they want to harm, but they're afraid to put their gun on the table. They'll be detractors within a community, but they won't threaten to harm anyone; harming anyone would mean risking their reputation and the life they've built. Their complaints are tepid and weak, and they'll make Statements that feel wishy-washy without committing to any one side, even the one representing the Fascist ideology. (Cowardly) Fascists have no ideology to speak of, keyboard warriors and a nuisance more than anything else.For the (Belligerent) Fascist, make them incomprehensible. Fascism is an ideology that fries the brains of anyone exposed to it. The (Belligerent) Fascist is caught in a feedback loop, isolated from their communities because of their intolerant views. They've been forced to communicate with other Fascists and lack any human connection, now that they are unwelcome in modern society. Now they cling tenuously to reality, and those few victims in their life who still hold hope in their hearts the Fascist can change.Fascists live in fear of losing any last scraps of human connection; wanting to avoid detection, they are forced to mask their speech, their worldview becomes incomprehensible strings of 'masked' speech. Slang for increasingly vile speech is slathered over their words as they learn more and more alien dialects of racism from Fascists in other countries; soon, a simple 'how do you do?' becomes 'am goon tak, mai brit!' Vocal centers break down into sludge, until Players who interact with the (Belligerent) Fascist are only vaguely aware that the person in front of them used to be human like them.Brainwashing at the root of human language is not difficult to undo; deprogramming the (Belligerent) Fascist only takes reintroducing them to society and making them live along normal people. Once they integrate into a community, they lose their speech impediments and can recover from their crippling social disease; until they've been cured, Directors can indicate that the character has expressed vile opinions, but nobody understood exactly what those words meant.For the (Depraved) Fascist, keep them action-oriented. (Depraved) Fascists have spent so long being (Belligerent) that they've lost their humanity and their soul. With not a single worldly connection, they are untethered from morality and joy; they will stop at nothing to see another creature suffer. No vile action they take can fill the hole in their heart, and that drives them madder still; wealth and fame can only briefly plug the void. They hunger for the suffering of others.The words of the (Depraved) Fascist are not important to the Narrative; their goal is to inflict injury and harm. Their vile deeds can be kept to rumors and whispers, to dossiers and bounty posters. When a (Depraved) Fascist is present, they don't need to speak at all, except to intimidate their next victims. The (Depraved) Fascist should start Conflict with insults and come ready to kill; they're not looking to engage in debate, they're looking to find a way to ruin their next victim and leave them in tatters.Whatever is easiest for the (Depraved) Fascist will be their goal. They will join onto bandwagons without believing in any of the words they say, slithering in and out of social groups like a snake. If they have a defenseless victim alone in a dark alleyway, they will act as if there are no consequences. But before they can cross that final line-- before they can next successfully assault, or demean, or kill-- Players should always be given a window to enjoin into Conflict with the (Depraved) Fascist. This is the best way to approach the (Depraved) Fascist; never give them a speech at all, only focus on their actions and the behaviors encouraged by their ideology.For the

neverland-system/theories/entropy.html.title: entropy
neverland-system/theories/entropy.html.description: Entropy is a real and fundamental force of the universe; everything will become simpler over time.

neverland-system/theories/holism.html.title: Holism
neverland-system/theories/holism.html.description:
Laconic: to consider the whole to be more than the parts.Holism is a curious concept sometimes employed during Arbitration and often employed when casting Cues; the Holist philosophy is to consider the whole of something as more than the sum of its parts. It is often appropriate to respond to a Prompt by indicating you are using a holistic method of spell-casting. You do not have to define what your Cues target exactly, especially when defining a spell would violate suspension of disbelief.Consider a bird; a bird contains a beak, talons, eyes, meat and feathers! But when it comes time to cast a spell to effect a 'bird', to what part of the bird does the spell apply? Does the spell apply only to the beak, or the heart, or does it apply to each feather individually? Obviously not. It's a spell for a bird. You didn't cast a spell targeting feathers, you cast the spell to effect the bird. Even if you did cast a spell to target the feathers, feathers are themselves holistic! Feathers have a vane, a shaft, barbs, an afterfeather and a quill. Barbs are holistic as well; barbs could contain many interlocking barbules, including a distal barbule, the ramus and the proximal barbule. Distal barbules are holistic; they're made with skin cells and bio-pigments. One of many bio-pigments found in birds is melanin; melanin is holistic, being a compound composed of oxygen, carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen. Oxygen is holistic; it is composed of electrons, protons and neutrons... and would you believe it, protons are also holistic! It's wholes all the way down!
Rather than engage in silly shenanigans every time someone casts a spell, we encourage Players to be Rule of Charitability and assume Strikes and Cues are applied in the most appropriate way to achieve the Caster's desired effect.

neverland-system/theories/inevitability-.title: Inevitability and Certainty
neverland-system/theories/inevitability-.description: Laconic: Hard to avoid.When a Statement is inevitable in the Neverlands System, it will always become Facts by the end of the Act during which that Statement is revealed. No Rule or Mechanic may alter the course of the events; (Inevitable) Statements must occur. With that said:(Inevitable) Statements may be interpreted in multiple ways.There are many 'prophecies' which seem to spell inevitable failure, but the listener lacked important context. An ominous promise that the 'sun will be devoured by the earth' may instead refer to the sun setting every night; the movements of windmills may be mistaken for deadly giants. A Statement which is (Inevitable) may be superseded by an Alternative interpretation of a Statement which says essentially the same thing, at the Director's discretion.

neverland-system/theories/locked-room-th.title: Locked Room Theory
neverland-system/theories/locked-room-th.description:
Discomfort warning; suicide and self-harm are briefly discussed.Locked room mysteries do not, in actuality, exist. The door can be locked, certainly; if it were not guarded by a locked door, then the premise would not be so intriguing. But a locked room? Such a thing has never existed. For a crime to take place in a locked room, there are three possibilities: The crime is inflicted by someone in the room who never leaves the room. The incident never occurred in the first place. The room is not locked.
Examining the first premise; self-harm is not a mystery to be solved. If an Actor were to do grievous self-harm to themselves in a locked room, then the Actor is exercising their bodily autonomy. This is a very real possibility, but it is the last possibility; to avoid triggering the Rule of Discomfort we encourage Directors to avoid using this premise in mysteries.That does, however, leave the possibility of a second culprit inside the locked room. Since there is no 'lock' preventing a second culprit from crawling out from underneath a desk, killing the victim and hiding underneath the desk again, out of sight of the investigators, there's no 'locked room' in this mystery at all, only a door that is locked from a single perspective.Then, we have the crimes that did not happen; the incidents which are imagined to be locked rooms, but are not locked rooms (or perhaps even incidents) in the first place. The corpse may have been placed in a locked room, then the incident made to look as if it was locked; you have no guarantee the crime took place inside the room. It is a possibility no crime took place at all, and the incident inside the locked room is imagined! If a victim died of coronary heart failure, we can hardly call that a murder.Next, we look at the unlocked locked room; a room with multiple passages in or out. If there is a window that can be opened, then it is not a sealed room; anyone could simply walk in or out of the room, opening the possibility of a second culprit; since a second culprit could exist, the 'mystery' of the locked room is solved. There are no locks, and it only appeared to be locked from the first perspective.

neverland-system/theories/response-windo.title: response window
neverland-system/theories/response-windo.description:
Laconic: A player's window to respond to a director declaring a statement is fact.The response window is a five-to-ten second window under which a challenge can be made to a Statement about to be canonized. The Neverlands System is designed to be quick and snappy; if the Directors needed to wait for Players before taking any action, it could be disruptive to play. Directors could be more lenient and allow Players additional time, especially if they're new to the Neverlands System, but eventually the Directors will need to take and complete actions to advance the Game-State.Directors who take an action should give a brief pause; if a Player raises their hand, or asks for the Director to wait, the Director should listen. Five to ten seconds is generous; Players are likely to respond before that time window is over, but if another Player cuts in with side chatter during the response window, the Directors should remain aware of nonverbal cues from their Players. Shy Players may need additional hand-holding until they can build the confidence to respond quickly and efficiently, especially over Players
On the other hand, this rule could be used to deter Players from attempting to disable or prevent other Players from acting. Asking for retroactive challenges may not be intentional or malicious, but the effects would be felt either way. Directors can simply tell a Player who acts outside the response window that they need to respond in a reasonable time-frame; as long as the Director gave adequate pause, the Statement will remain canonized.
Remember that Statements which make the Table uncomfortable should still be examined and potentially removed. When a Player feels uncomfortable, they might be unable to voice their thoughts until well outside the response window; asking to challenge a Statement because it makes them uncomfortable should be possible even when the response window has closed.

headers.heading: Note: Discomfort and the Response Window

neverland-system/theories/risk-and-dures.title: Risk and Duress
neverland-system/theories/risk-and-dures.description:
When Arbitration is performed under duress, that means that Cues are at greater risk of failure than normal. Cues performed under duress are automatically snuffed unless a Player or Actor takes precautions to to mitigate the risks.
What follows is a non-exhaustive list of Statuses under which a Cue is risky and one potential mitigation method. There are no shortage of unique cases or solutions that the we couldn't possibly imagine ourselves; risky situations not listed here is left as an exercise for the reader. Darkness could impede your tasks; a flashlight could beat away the darkness. Long hauls with heavy gear could leave you breathless; a short rest could help with fatigue. A carriage without fuel to operate may sputter and come to a halt; refueling could help. Carriages that run too long in the desert may overheat; coolant could keep the engine cool. Dashing across the room is only risky if you're out of shape. Stretch and exercise regularly! Addiction and alcoholism could cause internal conflict. Giving into alcoholism might mitigate the alcoholism, but it could also impair your judgement and make further actions harder. A problem gambler wouldn't have a problem if they left their wallet at home. Blurry vision is only an issue until you find and wear your glasses. It's easy to make a masterpiece in a studio designed to produce masterpieces. Performing that same action in a dingy alley surrounded by trash and scrap metal is another matter. Sparring with your master would be difficult and risky, but asking them to slow down and explain their techniques could help your comprehension greatly. Haste makes waste. Performing actions quickly is sure to make mistakes more common; slowing down would help, but it could also be a luxury you couldn't afford. Everything is riding on your success? That's a lot of pressure for one person to shoulder. Thankfully, you have your friends here to support you in your time of need! Doing the job of three men is difficult without three men to help you do the job! You've never fixed your own plumbing before? Thankfully you've got a toolbox and tutorials on the internet; imagine having to figure out plumbing from scratch! Building furniture is hard, but thankfully yours came with easy to follow blueprints. Right? It's hard to defend yourself in court; there's nothing wrong with calling on a professional. Making delicious food without a sense of taste is hard. Asking for a sous-chef sounds smart. Firing a rocket from a swerving car is sure to make a mess. Good thing you've got a laser sight and plenty of experience with rocketry. Down the helicopter goes! Keeping balance on a slippery surface can take all your focus, until you've put on the harness. It's a firefight? Good thing you found some durable cover. Concrete to the rescue! World's too loud for you? Pop on a pair of headphones and put on your favorite song. You have no idea what's going on, but listening to the brains of the crew has never steered you wrong. Do what they say and you don't need to know anything about the situation. This quiz is too hard-- thankfully you've smuggled in the answer sheet inside your pencil. Your friend likes the bottle a little too much. Out the window it goes-- no addiction risk now! This beautiful fae has you in quite a bind; what if you convince them to loosen their tail a bit? Fascist propaganda making your friend feel nervous? Tear it down! Out of sight, out of mind. So you've brought a knife to a gun fight? They've brought a gun to a knife fight-- close the distance and you're just as lethal as they were, if not more.

neverland-system/theories/rule-of-charit.title: Rule of Charitability
neverland-system/theories/rule-of-charit.description:
The Rule of Charitability is a corollary Rule to the Neverlands System: always assume mistakes are made out of haste, not malice.
What this means in practice is that there is a brief response window after any Statement is made that allows Players to 'take back' a previous Statement. Players may, in the heat of a moment, poorly phrase their Statement or leave a typo in that distorts the meaning of the Statement; they may signal to the other Players that they would like to melt their own Statement. As charitable and logical Players, Tables ought to give Players some amount of charitability for their actions and allow for the fix to go through, and Directors ought to close the Conflict State if necessary.
After a Player was given sufficient time to read their own Statement, and to make their fix, the Director should open the Conflict State and prevent further alteration; Players were given time to respond to their own Statements, so they should not be able to correct those Statements later! If a Player realizes they've made a grave mistake given new context minutes later, they may be out of luck; nothing short of Discomfort or extraordinary circumstances should allow them to melt that Statement now.

neverland-system/theories/rule-of-econom.title: Rule of Economies
neverland-system/theories/rule-of-econom.description:
The Rule of Economies is the concept that economies are used to keep Actors from subverting Accords. There are rules that need to be followed for an Actor to collect their allotment of Breath from the nearby Dragon-Temple, and an Actor's actions should only be restricted if they threaten the Accords.
Think of it from a writing perspective; why should the Table not be allowed to collect as much wealth as possible? Well, with that wealth they could easily hire others to do the hard work for them. Their Actors could sit back and enjoy the benefits of the work other Actors are putting in, with the Narrative reduced to raw mechanical reports from their subordinates.
That's not to say you can't make this style of play interesting; a group of nobles managing a kingdom and its workers could be just as thrilling as playing a team of adventurers. But if that style of play subverts attention from the Accords, and the Accords form the backbone of the Narrative, then the Directors have a problem which requires solving.The Rule of Economies works to make this narratively interesting; goods are divided into negligible expenses, affordable expenses, lifestyle expenditures, apocalyptic expenditures and economic ventures.
Negligible expenses would slow down the Narrative if they were to be tallied up; unless an Actor is so poor they could barely afford a matchbox, we can assume that some meals and shelter are covered by their earnings or savings. It would slow down the pace of the game to assume otherwise; unless the Narrative specifically concerns an Actor's wealth that day, or a Player wishes to engage in impoverishment play, ignore these expenses. Cults usually provide all of these benefits as Assets and produce them with Geomancy, so there's no concern about these goods running out quickly.
Affordable expenses are expenses which are luxurious but not outside of an Actor's means. Buying a movie ticket, paying for a phone bill or building a comfortable pair of boots are usually not against the Accords of the local Dragon-Temple. Any Mortal who saved even part of their paycheck should be able to make these payments; they are negligible. Affordable does not mean accessible; these goods will require some effort to procure, though not enough effort to invoke risk.
Lifestyle expenditures are general markers for 'quality of life'; food, shelter, entertainment. Lifestyle expenditures are an Actor's way of improving their lot in life; to improve their lifestyle, they will need to get a promotion at work, patch up the drafty hole in their wall, or become a better hunter. Paying for a lifestyle expenditure should be a reward that Directors hand out for subverting an Apocalypse; adding a new ice cream machine to theirSanctuary to thank the Table for taking on Fascists is the polite and expected behavior. Players should notify their Directors that they want to spend an Act working on improving their Actor's lifestyle before making any expenditures of this value. Directors should be willing to give one or two lifestyle improvements an Act, especially if any Dances concern Dragon's Breath.
Apocalyptic expenditures are short-term goals; they're effectively heists, a fight over a resource or benefit that inherently subverts the Accords of the local Dragon-Temples. These are rare and powerful artifacts, or the deeds to million-dollar buildings; at least one Apocalypse will be necessary to secure these goods for the Table. All apocalyptic expenditures are opposed; even if no Cult took interest in the goods before the Table did, a Director should pick an existing Cult to make a rival play for the goods the Table seeks. Likewise, if a Cult seeks an artifact that Act, the Table should be given an opportunity to subvert their apocalyptic expenditure.
Economic ventures are long-term goals for the Table that cannot simply be broken down into single purchases. Funding a rebellion isn't cheap, and it's not the kind of thing that can be done over a single Act; it will require painstaking coordination and several apocalyptic expenditures to execute well. These are the goals that the Table sets to overwhelm Fascists; they are not simple expenses an Actor should be able to pay for with money. Economic ventures inherently subvert the Accords of a Dragon-Temple, and so some negotiation with Priestesses should be expected as part of the process of making the purchase and these ventures should be carefully scrutinized by the Directors. Don't just plot out financial incentives on a graph; try to make it fun for the Table, and try to break down the major barriers into four objectives: acquiring Scales to protect the investment, getting the approval of the local Priestesses, and organizing the Cult to guard the new venture. Lastly, start an Apocalypse to finalize the deal!

neverland-system/theories/rule-of-magica.title: Rule of Magical Thinking
neverland-system/theories/rule-of-magica.description:
The Threads that bind cause and effect are not always clear.From a certain perspective, at all times each and every atom in our body is pulled or pushed by its electrons against every particle of air and surface it comes into contact with; we could say that by seating ourselves against a chair, a chair was having some impact upon our mood. Could it be, then, that by pressing a cup against your chair, and then against your skin, would it be different from pressing a cup directly against your skin? Would we consider that by two objects coming into contact with one another they have been 'changed' in some way, the same way mixing flour and water can create dough and change the physical properties of the water?
Magical thinking is the attribution of cause and effect to more than that which we can see, extending into extrasensory causes; this cup and that cup are different, because one is clearly possessed of magic and has magical influences upon us. That sock was worn when an Actor witnessed their team winning the Superbowl, so that sock is the lucky sock. That Actor was present when the farm went through a terrible blight, so she must be a witch.
Superstition is a powerful thing; if an Actor can identify that your opponent believes in superstition, then that Actor could use their belief their Opinions are valid as proof that other Opinions are valid; that their use of a magic Inkwell means other Actors can dip into their magic Inkwell.
The Rule of Magical Thinking applies whenever an Actor uses magic, spells or believes in a superstition, Actors may posit that to disprove their belief, they must discard their own belief in magical thoughts. Forcing someone into a non-magical Conflict is risky and dangerous, so all enjoined parties to a Conflict must first agree that they are willing to melt all their (Cast) Statements before they can use this Rule to disable all magic in the Conflict.

neverland-system/theories/theory-of-clar.title: Theory of Clarity
neverland-system/theories/theory-of-clar.description:
There are at times duplicate or redundant terms for the same concept in the Neverlands System. This is not an attempt to pad the system or confuse the Table with additional vocabulary to memorize; this is a deliberate attempt on the part of the System to make some vocabulary easier to memorize, especially when these terms are used adjacent in text. Consider the following Statement:
'The Prop cast a Cue to pocket the Prop to relocate from the Position across a Thread to relocate to the next Position so the Prop could cast a Cue to pocket the Prop onto the new Position.'
This is a valid Statement in the Neverlands System, and there's no problem parsing this if you go slowly. Someone picked something up, carried it across the room, and set that something down. But it's confusing to read; it's a word teaser. What if, instead, we used a few clarifying terms?
'The Actor pocketed a Prop, relocated to that Position, and dropped that Prop.'Much easier to read! Sometimes, clarifying terms are employed when we've determined a term is too saturated in the System, such as how the term Position is sometime used instead of Statement. For these clarifying terms, we try to make sure that the use case for the new term is fairly consistent.Sometimes, clarifying terms are employed when a series of actions can be implied rather than executed step-by-step. Crossing a Prop means to relocate it between two or more Positions; it's easier to say 'cross' than describe the physical, redundant process of relocating something twice or more.
Lastly, clarifying terms are used when an action can have two different meanings. Players might not know you can pocket something to drop it, so the 'drop' Cue exists so players can intuitively guess their next Cue.
Sometimes, when reading an article one might stumble across a Laconic form provided for that article; this is a clarifying term designed to help Directors who are more informed quickly convey a mechanic to less informed Players!

neverland-system/theories/theory-of-fun..title: Theory of Fun
neverland-system/theories/theory-of-fun..description:
The Theory of Fun says that if something isn't fun or at least interesting, the Table should look at ways to make it fun. There's a common adage that everything is more interesting with explosions. Long biology lectures might not be interesting to the Table, but if ninjas with guns interrupt the lecture and turn it into a hostage situation, Players now have the opportunity to turn a learning moment into an action scene. Alternatively, the Table might find a horde of sword-wielding Samurai boring after weeks of slogging through the Vulcan Constellation; maybe turning this fight into a lecture on Vulcanite biology and culture might be more engaging!
These Theories are to taste, since too much of one Element or another risks causing different Players at the Table discomfort. Understanding the other Players is a soft skill that may take years to fully develop, but keeping an open line of communication is important; Players should let the Directors know when they're not having fun, and the Directors should frequently ask Players about their favorite moments so far. Downtimes are great for checking in on Players, since there's no risk of Conflict during Downtime.
Players should not feel afraid to ask the Directors to make a Scene more interesting. These Theories could be used mechanically to ask the Directors to enjoin additional Actors to a Conflict or to stake the stakes of the Scene; if the story isn't interesting enough, it probably needs the additional flavor.
Just remember: these Theories are to taste! Make sure the whole Table consents before invoking it. Players might be having fun without the additional spice.

neverland-system/theories/theory-of-hone.title: Theory of Honesty
neverland-system/theories/theory-of-hone.description:
Laconic: The Table should never lie to other Players about the game state.There are few faster ways to cause Discomfort than to lie to someone else. It's more than just telling Fiction; intentionally misrepresenting the State of Canon is cruel, if not unethical. Imagine that a Player is doing everything right; they use the optimal Cues at the perfect time, they've done extensive research and pieced together a ten-minute long argument to defeat Fascism once and for all.
Just as the Table casts their final Cue, the Director taps the shoulder of the Player, revealing that their arguments were actually all based on lies, so each one of the Cues they played that relied on Statements being Facts now fails in Arbitration. The Table loses the Conflict, the Fascists throw them all in prison, and everyone feels like this whole Conflict was a miserable waste of their time. It would not be shocking to see that Player quit soon after!This is the consequence of dishonest play!
As a Rule, whenever a Player speaks in Fiction, they need to announce that they are lying or playing a Cue which enables them to speak in Fiction. Players should always denote Fiction, and Directors who want to tell a mystery should use Alternatives to present duplicitous Statements instead of labeling them as Facts. Directors should get in the habit of denoting any Statement with a remote possibility of being a lie as Fiction, Alternatives or even Opinions.
Some readers are probably balking at the idea of revealing a mystery to Players. 'What do you mean, the existence of a locked room is mere Opinion? Isn't that as good as admitting there is no locked room in this whodunnit?'
To these readers, I speak plainly; there is no such thing as a Locked Room Theory. Beyond that, Players are writers coallaborating towards a grander Narrative; there must be no deception between collaborators. If your mystery cannot be told without gaslighting your friends, your mystery is not worth reading. As a mystery writer, the first rule of mysteries is to foreshadow every clue; to clearly spell out each of the suspects, so nobody is surprised; to use dramatic irony to keep the audience in suspense.
If a Scene seems too obvious, subvert expectations; supply just enough information that any of the alternative hypotheses could be true! An excellent Director could even have both possibilities exist as equally valid Alternatives, and let the Players decide which version of reality makes the most sense; you are not the only writer at the Table, and relying on Players can at times make for incredibly satisfying conclusions.

neverland-system/appendix.html.title: Appendix
neverland-system/appendix.html.description: All of these are open-source textbooks in English designed to get Players familiar with entry-level philosophy, physics, mathematics, social studies, writing, medicine, history and science. Since the Neverlands System relies so heavily on logical debate and fact, knowing basic facts about our own world can help play proceed quickly and efficiently. We encourage Players to try and understand more about our world and seek out even more information; the more you know, the richer your understanding of the world and her miracles will be.Thank you for spending time with us, in this world. We hope you find joy and meaning in the fight against fascist ideology, and the skills you learn here can be applied in your everyday life.

neverland-system/astral-sea.html.title: Astral Sea
neverland-system/astral-sea.html.description:
The Astral Sea, otherwise known as outer space, is the vast Stage upon which the Constellations rest. The Astral Sea is a cold, hard vacuum. There are no sounds and there is no heat, because phonons are oscillations of molecules in sequence; molecule density is so thin that oscillations would be nearly imperceptible. It is densely irradiated; photons travel properly through the vacuum of space, exciting any molecules they touch. Since space has an extremely low density, it also has a low pressure, and the pressure of space decreases the boiling points of many materials. In space, since the boiling point of blood is lowered and photons excite any material they touch, contact with light is deadly; the blood wouldn't evaporate right out of your frozen skin, not quickly, but measures must be taken to protect oneself in space.
Mortals wear space suits (or Costumes with good Hazard defenses) to isolate themselves from space; inside the suit, there is a relatively 'normal' pressure, no exposure to light and survivable warmth. This pressure extends to their space shuttles (Carriages) and space stations (Sanctuaries), which are meticulously designed spaces to keep an astronauts blood from boiling. Breathable air, nourishment, warmth and pressure are provided to Mortals within these spaces, allowing them to survive even in these inhospitable conditions.
Planets (Constellations) are extremely far apart; far enough entire generations could breed and perish in the time it takes to reach another solar system. There are no known or proven ways to fold space like a pretzel at time of writing, though it would be convenient for space colonization.
The Astral Sea is a literal sea, and any competent captain can ferry a crew in her Dragon-Temple to relocate their cargo between Constellations. Dragon-Temples are the preferred Carriages of the Astral sea, because they can properly supply the crew with Breath on their long journeys. Dragon-Temples also provide a Cult for the crew to center themselves around; fights and violence would often break out without the cohesion a religion provides.
Each Constellation in the Astral Sea sits further and further away from the last, but the Neverlands are much closer than that; by the time the subsequent generations of Cultists reach the Neverlands centuries later, they should be delusional enough to comprehend their many wonders. Competent Priestesses and Divines could use magical thinking to shorten the journey to months, or within the crew's lifespan at least; smarter Mages and Mortals would prefer to travel to local Constellations instead, isles only a few months or years from Earth. There are, after all, millions of Dragon-Temples to explore in our own Solar System!

neverland-system/candles.html.title: Candles
neverland-system/candles.html.description: Laconic: A Major Dynamic of Prop which greatly modifies Results.

neverland-system/conflict-state-handbook.title: Conflict State Handbook
neverland-system/conflict-state-handbook.description:
For those familiar with social cues in roleplay, this handbook may feel redundant at times. As a form of best practice, the Neverlands System never assumes familiarity with any subject, and each part of the book is designed for readers of all experience levels. When it comes to the Conflict State, Players and Directors will both need to intuit when the Conflict State is open* or closed. Conflicts are only expected to take between fifteen and thirty minutes each, so the more experienced Players are with social cues the smoother Conflict will beome.In an (Open) State, we expect Players at the Table to be examining the Statements in play. (Open) State are rarely quiet-- they're filled with questions and banter, hands reaching in and out of the Rack and adding to its contents. Players need time to read, to think over their arguments and to make new Statements for the Rack. Directors need time to mull over the consequences of the actions players take and formulate their own arguments. We recommend Directors try to reach an (Open) State as often as possible, since an (Open) State gives both Players and Directors the freedom they need to make dramatic reveals and fancy strikes.An (Open) State occurs anytime there isn't a reason to call for a (Closed) State. A (Closed) State occurs when it would make more sense to prevent Players from making new Neverlands System operates differently from other games in its genre. Typical games use a 'turn' format to determine who can act in an (Open) State. With each Player waiting around and doing nothing while combat proceeds, Players of these systems are intended to use that time to figure out their next move. We've found that giving Players real-world downtime tends to lead them to distraction and disinterest, and those two factors slow down the pace of the game dramatically. We intend for all Players to be fully engaged at all times during Conflict, and so we've designed the (Open) State to keep players on their toes.
Remember that the Order of Conflict Resolution suggests that Directors ask the Table if they need to grab snacks, food, or use the restroom before Conflict formally begins. Directors need about three to five minutes to to establish the Stage and Lines in Canon, so Players are encouraged to use the (Closed) State that Conflict opens with to make refills or calls. Taking the time now to meet your physical needs will save the whole Table time later during conflict; Players should not expect to have a moment's rest during Conflict, as giving the other Players an extra ten minutes to examine the Statements in Conflict can lead to an extremely unfair advantage. Nobody wants to be caught flat-footed because they were in the restroom while someone discovered the true culprit of a murder mystery.

neverland-system/constellations.html.title: Constellations
neverland-system/constellations.html.description:
Laconic: An island in a sea of madness, a place to rest your boots.The Constellations (also known as the Neverlands or the Myriad Constellations) are the home of the Neverlanders, magical places seated in the Astral Sea (aka the depths of outer space). The Constellations are abstractions, islands that only exist if you believe in them strongly enough. Legends say that N'as filled the Constellations with Neverlanders by accident; artificial creatures born purely from imagination, they formed when she tried to imagine a world herself. Thankfully, whatever force animates these creatures doesn't depend on her attention-- brave Mortals and brazen Elves continued to breed in her absence, introducing millions of inhabitants across the Myriad Constellations.
Constellations lack borders; when they need more land for their citizens, they simply claim additional Statements until there are new lands. Most citizens live within Dragon-Temples, massive egregores that protect them from the harsh environment of the Astral Sea. If claiming Statements proves too slow to accomplish their goals, Constellations will entreat their Dragon-Temples to breed and make smaller settlements that citizens can live inside for now.
Constellations develop cultures and technologies independently of one another; they are not averse to working with foreigners, but technology that works well in one Cult may fail in a different cult. Since magic is based in belief, societies must believe that foreign spellcraft works in ways that make sense to their cultural identity.
Each society worships their Dragon-Temples in different ways; most often, Constellations form pantheons. Citizens of the Dragon-Temples are tasked to pay tribute to their home Dragon-Temple, with each Dragon-Temple demanding its own form of tribute. As such, the local economies of Constellations tend to reflect the special interests of local Dragon-Temples. A Dragon-Temple who demands famous works of art as tribute invariably finds herself in the company of talented artists.
Constellations are also home to their own unique blends of Flora and Fauna. The Breaths of each Dragon-Temple in the Constellation are often related to one another, descendants of the first Neverlanders who chose to breed with Mortals. As such, they tend to be a blend of fantasy and reality; examples include agaric caps with little hats, rabbits with unicorn horns, and clockwork golems in the shape of a door. Mortals should find these creatures both recognizable and alien, as each operate in ways unique to that Constellation's Lines.
Every Constellation has established their own unique Lines called Accords detailing how they function; the rules of physics which make them different from other Constellations, the way they perceive magic and how magic operates. Tables are encouraged to improvise Accords and make adjustments as necessary to make their Narrative work; maybe one Line isn't true today because the story the party wants to tell is different.
Each Cult understands magic differently, and each Constellation is shaped by that understanding of magic. The Flora and Fauna that spring forth from a Constellation's Inkwell have properties of the native Neverlanders as well as Mortal counterparts and obey the Accords of the Constellations. The people have an economy and religion built around the same Dragon-Temples they live inside; all of this comes together to shape a fantastic setting for any campaign!
Disclaimer: If you wish to use any of the Lore elements discussed in this half of the rulebook for personal or commercial purposes, you've got the green light from us. Be sure to check the Attributions and Licenses page for more information about our policies, but all of these settings are provided to the public for free for use in any project that meets Community Standards.

neverland-system/council-of-knots.html.title: Council of Knots
neverland-system/council-of-knots.html.description:
The Council of Knots is a Cult dedicated to the death of the Goddess of Suffering, N'as. The Council believes that by killing the Goddess, they can end the Cycle of Suffering, the Constellations will be free and Mortals will all be able to cast Cues. They believe that the Goddess oppresses the free will of Mortals, and that it is the ultimate right of Mortals to express their free will at any expense; they will murder, rob, mangle and jaywalk in their pursuit of their agenda.
This is not to say that the Council is uncaring; independent Councilors are likely to be friendly and personable, and in general the Council will aid the Table in uprooting Fascist Cults. They care deeply about their cause and will always side with the oppressed over the oppressors.With that said, many Councilors are themselves selfish and aggressive; they will happily abuse the fear and kindness of others to get their foot in the door. Councilors are known to act as terrorists, setting fires to buildings and planting bombs in populated areas. They don't respect the personal space of others and assume that they are the 'main character' at all times; if their personal Cult is not deathly afraid of them, they must be new Councilors.
The head Councilor is known as the Enemy of the World. The Enemy of the World is an inherited position; when the previous Enemy of the World is bested, a new Councilor may take the mantle. Mysten follows the Enemy of the World religiously, as if they had a pact together; Seprix also takes special interests in the activities of the Enemy of the World. The Enemy of the World is, perhaps, the only person in the galaxy capable of threatening N'as and ending her eternal life once and for all.Nobody has been successful. Yet. But certainly, the Council of Knots will try...
Councilors have few Assets as a rule; they travel light by necessity. Most Councilors are fugitives and terrorists, so they're unlikely to be able to stay in Dragon-Temples for long. Councilors usually carry around their Sanctuary in their Pockets-- they live inside their favored mode of transport, never staying in one place for long unless they've found an interesting scheme to play out.
Councilors are expected to provide affordable services to Mages and Mortals who seek them out; they are expected to grant the wishes of the foolish and the damned, though their Blessings are risky and likely to backfire. It is a good policy to expect a Councilor to grant a wish for 'free', only for the price to be something the Actor was never going to be willing to pay. The Council of Knots will always provide advice and services to the accursed, helping them adapt to their new lifestyle, though that advice may simply be nagging and philosophy at best.
The Council of Knots don't hold any Dragon-Temples, but they are talented at securing abandoned buildings and embedding themselves in a community without being noticed. Some Councilors are known to brazenly manipulate the minds of Mortals, turning them into obedient thralls. Most thralls are or were Fascists, so the Council turns a blind eye to these activities; still, Councilors who engage in acts of mental enslavement are likely to be punished and reproached by other Councilors once made aware.

neverland-system/cycle-of-suffering.html.title: Cycle of Suffering
neverland-system/cycle-of-suffering.html.description:
The Cycle of Suffering is the process by which Mortals become Mages, Echoes and Divines. It is a process which is mediated by the Goddess of Suffering, N'as and which has taken many forms over the centuries, with each Dragon-Temple and Cult defining their own Cycle.
Firstly, it is a universal proposition that all Actors experience suffering. The amount of suffering Actors experience is not equal; some number of Actors suffer more than others. Actors who suffer the most fall below the second root of the variance between all suffering Actors; they fall outside the standard deviation. Actors who fall outside the range of standard deviation are eligible to become Mages without paying an additional price to do so.
Mages are capable of casting Cues and teach other Mortals to become Mages themselves.
