Among their number, a few appeal to the sensibilities of Neverlanders; these Mages are able to enter pacts with these ancient species and become Divines.Divines are the combination of Neverlander(s) and a Mage; they are Mages with more magical and spiritual capabilities than their peers. It is rare, though not unheard of, for a Divine to be formed from someone who does not fall outside the standard deviation of suffering. The process of Tempering is painful; the body is purged of its pollutants and sins in an agonizing, hours-long process facilitated by the Goddess of Suffering. Once all impurities have been purged, the Divine must go through Tempering every so often to remove new impurities from their body; especially nasty Divines who live in modern society may have stronger reactions to Tempering than those who live ascetic and quiet lives.
Divines and Mages who pass away will form Echoes; these are Neverlanders who represent their essence and carry their memories into the future. Some Echoes choose to undergo Reincarnation and become Mortals again, following the plans of the God of Suffering; others choose to be guided by the Five-River Ferrymen to the Lunar Constellation, where they live out their eternity in a run-down 'paradise'. When a Mortal drinks the blood of god, they may perish immediately and become an Echo; this process is called syndication and their victims are colloquially known as the Syndicated.

neverland-system/diresk.html.title: Diresk
neverland-system/diresk.html.description:
Diresk is the second Sephirah, one facet of the Goddess of Suffering, N'as. He is the spirit of discovery; he travels the world in search of new and interesting sights, observing and relocating to new Positions at a steady pace.
When attempting to blend in with Mortals, Diresk takes the form of a large and muscular man with a well-traveled complexion. His hair is fashioned into dreadlocks with beads interwoven between the braids,

neverland-system/echoes.html.title: Echoes
neverland-system/echoes.html.description: Laconic: The ghostly residue of a dead Mage.

neverland-system/elves.html.title: Elves
neverland-system/elves.html.description: Laconic: Any native of a Constellation (besides Earth).

neverland-system/english.html.title: English
neverland-system/english.html.description: Laconic: One or more Translations made by Scribes is in this language.

neverland-system/game-mechanics.html.title: Game Mechanics
neverland-system/game-mechanics.html.description:
Disambiguation: Narrative Elements, Notes, Scripts and Rules all lead here.There are game mechanics in the Neverland System that Players are likely to engage with through the course of play.
Narrative Elements are physical structures that can be interacted with by the Players. In the Neverlands System, the word Element (short for Narrative Element) is always capitalized, as are all Elements, to help players easily identify Elements by sight. In this particular version of the Neverlands System, the first time an Element is referenced in an article it will also have a link to the page describing what that Element is and how to interpret Statements with those Elements.Rules are metaphysical properties that explain how Elements behave under different scenarios. In the Neverlands System, Rules are not capitalized, but they are meta-narrative Statements about different Elements and the recommended way to play with those Elements. Rules are not etched in stone; if a Director determines that a Rule would be uncomfortable to implement or harmful to Players for any reason, they are encouraged to bend and rewrite Rules. As a general rule (pun intended), Rules should make the Neverland System easier to comprehend and allow Players to resolve conflicts in a timely and effective manner.What follows is a list of each category of Narrative Element or Rule that exists within the Neverland System. Directors are encouraged to understand the existing Narrative Elements and Rules before finding new ones. These categories are rather broad and useful for all kinds of stories, and should only require minimal adaptation to fit the Table's needs.
Players who struggle to understand the following index are encouraged to visit the Appendix and read the section on Entry-Level Open Source Textbooks. There's no shame in needing to learn basic logic, language arts or storytelling techniques before playing.
Arbitration Arbitration is the mechanism by which the Director determines a Statement's kind or veracity.
Statements Statements are phrases that can be logically determined as Fact, Fiction, Opinion or a Question.
Positions A Position is a Statement which puts forward a claim to Fact and can be validated through logic.
Cards Cards include any element which contain or display Statements and can be placed at a Position.
Conflicts
Narratives
Players Players include any agents capable of autonomous thought engaging with game mechanics.
Racks Racks house every Statement which has been posed by a Player but not logically validated yet.
Actors Actors are Narrative Elements which are voiced by Players and act according to Player wishes.
Pockets

neverland-system/narrative-awareness-sys.title: Narrative Awareness System
neverland-system/narrative-awareness-sys.description:
There exists an entity which, to our best knowledge, created the universe. She doesn't claim to have created the universe, but we know she is responsible for it as the Goddess of Suffering, N'as. N'as is the source of all magic in the Neverlands System, and whether or not she exists is a decision for the Table to make; most Narratives function just fine without her presence. She is, for all intents and purposes, immortal, indestructible and indefatigable. N'as is unable and unwilling to enjoin Conflict, and she can only act with honesty. The Table should not be concerned about her interference, as unless bothered she is content to sit back and witness the Narrative.
Everyone knows N'as by different names; her Sephirot represent her different aspects, each have a different appearance in the minds of her adherents. To the Elves, she is a massive tree with a singular eye; to the Constellations, she is the land and the sea. The Dragon-Temples view her as a threat to their power, and the Neverlanders live inside her memory. To the Earthlings, they know her only by her Blessings and her many guises.But they all consider N'as the God of Suffering.N'as

neverland-system/neverlanders.html.title: Neverlanders
neverland-system/neverlanders.html.description:
A Neverlander is an egregore; they are thoughts given physical form and manifested into our world. Thought-forms like Neverlanders are flexible things; they assume a new and different Blessing from N'as during Intermissions, taking on different roles inside the Narrative as the plot demands. Neverlanders are generally ageless creatures; they cannot be killed as long as the idea they are based upon continues to exist.
Neverlanders derive more than their attitude and personality from their base concept; they also derive power from that concept's popularity. The fear of death, for example, might produce a powerful Neverlander with the touch of death or the visage of a Grim Reaper. Neverlanders thankfully reside in the Astral Sea, living on Constellations narratively far from Earth... but there are exceptions to the rule.
Sometimes, these Neverlanders formed from negative emotions spill out into the Mortal Realm, forcing Mages to fight them back and force them back into the Neverlands. At times, these Mages form pacts with Neverlanders; these pacts result in the creation of Divines, powerful half-Neverlanders with a Mortal body. Divines are not necessarily a single individual; they may be made of a Neverlander providing direct assistance to their Mage counterpart, and may only be connected by a narrative Thread.
Neverlanders do not consider their lives to be worth much, if anything at all; they are very willing to die or perish for a time to sell a gag. They do value the lives of Mortals greatly, however; devouring a Mortal can produce a Divine without a pact. Neverlanders who completely devour their Mortal halves or contract with a dead Mortal are Echoes, pale imitations of the real thing left to join the Cycle of Suffering or rot in the Lunar Constellation.
Ideas of Actors are usually not just Neverlanders, but mirror-demons from the Catron Constellation. They are incredible fans of the person they are based on, to the point of stalking and collecting all the memorabilia of that individual; when an Actor perishes, these Neverlanders get first dibs at becoming their Echo. If an Actor has freed their mirror-demon, as a Councilor might, they no longer have a reflection.

neverland-system/neverlands-system.html.title: Neverlands System
neverland-system/neverlands-system.html.description: The Neverlands System is a gamified system of organization for narrative elements. Using the mechanics of the Neverland System, a group of one or more writers can sit at the same table and write a story where fairies fight fascists and mages form socialist communes. The magic of the Neverlands System is based on emotions, while the themes of the Neverlands System are based in mythology and popular fiction. It's a kitchen sink setting with a defined core that Players can play through if they wish, with rules designed to make running the setting simple for the Directors and combat fun and frantic. All Players can always act at the same time, engage with the same systems and leave their own unique impact on the Narrative that forms.

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.
